title: "I Didn't Know Where to Turn"—How One Family Found Help After a House Fire
bodyHeavy:
When a kitchen fire displaced the Nguyen family from their Brooklyn Center apartment earlier this winter, they had no renters insurance and no relatives nearby. With two school-age children and a newborn, Linh Nguyen spent the first night at a hotel paid for by the Red Cross, unsure what would happen next.

A caseworker at Hennepin County connected the family with Community Emergency Assistance Programs, better known as CEAP, which has operated out of Brooklyn Center for more than forty years. Within 48 hours, staff had secured a furnished two-bedroom unit through a landlord partnership, delivered a crib and kitchen basics, and enrolled the family in emergency SNAP benefits. A benefits navigator sat with Linh through every application — medical assistance, child care assistance, utility emergency aid — and followed up by phone each week.

The children returned to school within a week. The infant's pediatric appointments were rescheduled to a clinic within walking distance of the new unit. Linh, who had been working part-time as a dental assistant, was referred to a workforce counselor to help her add hours without jeopardizing her benefits eligibility. Six weeks after the fire, the family signed a longer-term lease on the same unit.

"I kept waiting for somebody to hand me a list of phone numbers," Linh said. "Instead they made the calls with me."

CEAP's housing navigator program has served more than 300 families since it launched, with the majority moving into permanent housing within ninety days. Staff credit a growing landlord partnership network and a small emergency fund that can cover security deposits on short notice.
bodyMedium: A kitchen fire left the Nguyen family homeless in Brooklyn Center. Within 48 hours, CEAP helped them find temporary housing, furnishings, and a benefits navigator who walked them through every application.
bodyLight: How one Brooklyn Center family found help from CEAP after a house fire displaced them.

meta.deck: After a kitchen fire left them homeless, the Nguyen family found a community that showed up—not with a list of numbers, but with real help.

title: Thousands Expected at Region's Largest Winter Food Drive
bodyHeavy:
Over 40 organizations will converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center this weekend for the annual Fill the Shelves winter food drive. Organizers expect more than 3,000 attendees and hope to collect 50,000 pounds of non-perishable food items — roughly twice last year's haul, which staff attributed to a milder December that softened demand at the pantries they serve.

This year's event comes as food shelves across the metro report record intake. Second Harvest Heartland, the region's largest food bank, has warned partner agencies that winter reserves are running thin. Volunteers from across the metro have signed up to sort, pack, and distribute donations to food shelves in all five core metro counties, with priority routing to pantries in Brooklyn Center, Richfield, and the East Side of St. Paul that have seen the steepest spikes in first-time visitors.

Most-needed items, according to the coordinating committee, are shelf-stable protein, low-sodium canned vegetables, cooking oil, and culturally specific staples such as masa harina, basmati rice, and halal canned meat. Organizers will also accept unopened hygiene supplies, which are not covered by SNAP.

Free parking is available in the Convention Center ramp, and Metro Transit is offering free rides on Routes 5, 14, and 22 for anyone showing a Fill the Shelves flyer. Doors open in the morning and the drive runs through late afternoon.
bodyMedium: Over 40 organizations converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center this weekend for the annual Fill the Shelves drive, expecting 3,000+ attendees and 50,000 pounds of food donations to restock metro-area pantries.
bodyLight: Annual Fill the Shelves food drive this weekend at the Convention Center.

meta.deck: Over 40 organizations converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center for the annual Fill the Shelves drive, with priority routing to pantries seeing the steepest spikes in demand.

title: How a Block Club in Phillips Organized Its Own Warming Station
bodyHeavy:
When the city ran out of warming center capacity during a bitter cold snap this winter, neighbors on 25th and Bloomington Avenue opened their church basement. Temperatures that week bottomed out at 22 below, and the nearest municipal warming site had been closed for the night when a resident first called the block club's group text late on a Tuesday evening.

Within three days they had space heaters, donated blankets, a rotating schedule of volunteers, and enough hot coffee to keep fifty people warm overnight. Donations of socks, hand warmers, and instant oatmeal came from households within four square blocks; a neighbor who owns a cleaning company brought an industrial-size urn of coffee each evening. The church, which shares the corner with an auto body shop and a Somali mall, contributed fuel for the heaters and left the side door unlocked.

The block club coordinated with Minneapolis Emergency Management and Hennepin County outreach workers to ensure guests could connect with shelter referrals by morning. Two county staffers began rotating through the basement to do intake and schedule follow-up appointments — a handoff that organizers say kept the space from becoming a de facto shelter with no exit.

It started as an act of desperation, organizers said, but could become a model for neighborhood-level emergency response. The block club is now drafting a memorandum of understanding with the county so the protocol can activate faster next winter, with pre-staged supplies and a shared SMS tree that reaches both organizers and county outreach staff.
bodyMedium: When the city ran out of warming center capacity, neighbors on 25th and Bloomington opened their church basement and kept fifty people warm overnight. It started as desperation and became a model.
bodyLight: Phillips neighbors opened a church basement warming station during the cold snap.

meta.deck: When the city couldn't keep up, neighbors on 25th & Bloomington opened their church basement—now they're drafting a protocol with the county.

title: Point-in-Time Count Shows 12% Drop in Unsheltered Homelessness
bodyHeavy:
The annual point-in-time count found 12% fewer people sleeping outside statewide compared to last year, though advocates caution the improvement is partly due to expanded shelter capacity rather than permanent housing placements.

The count, coordinated by Wilder Research and conducted on a single night each winter, sends teams of volunteers into encampments, transit stations, and other public spaces to document people sleeping outside. Shelter operators separately report their overnight census. The combined snapshot drives state and federal funding formulas and shapes priorities for the coming year.

Wilder researchers cautioned that the statewide drop masks wide regional variation. Unsheltered counts rose in Greater Minnesota and in several outer-ring suburbs where shelter capacity has not kept pace with demand. Advocates point to sustained investments in emergency shelter beds but warn that without more permanent supportive housing, the decline could reverse as shelters fill up.
bodyMedium: Annual point-in-time count shows 12% fewer unsheltered Minnesotans, though advocates note expanded shelters—not permanent housing placements—account for much of the drop.
bodyLight: Statewide unsheltered homelessness down 12% in annual point-in-time count.

title: Minnesota Resettles 1,200 Afghan Refugees—What's Next
bodyHeavy:
Over the past several years, Minnesota has resettled more than 1,200 Afghan evacuees through a network of resettlement agencies. Many families are now transitioning off temporary assistance, and agencies report growing demand for employment services and English language classes.

The International Institute of Minnesota, one of four primary resettlement agencies in the state, reports that most adult arrivals have now been enrolled in workforce training or English language programs. Housing remains the hardest-to-secure service — agency staff say rental affordability in the metro has been the biggest barrier to placing families in long-term housing near jobs and schools.

Community partners including CLUES, the Advocates for Human Rights, and the Immigrant Law Center are handling the legal casework as arrivals apply for permanent status. Readers who want to help can contact any of the four resettlement agencies directly about volunteer tutoring, donated household goods, or cash donations to fill gaps federal funding does not cover.
bodyMedium: Minnesota has resettled over 1,200 Afghan refugees. Agencies now report growing demand for employment services, English language classes, and affordable housing near jobs.
bodyLight: Over 1,200 Afghan refugees resettled in Minnesota since 2021.

title: Twin Cities Urban Tree Canopy Drops 8% Since 2015
bodyHeavy:
A new analysis of aerial imagery and ground surveys found that the Twin Cities metro area has lost roughly 8% of its urban tree canopy over the past decade, largely due to emerald ash borer and drought stress. The steepest losses are concentrated in North Minneapolis and parts of East St. Paul, where canopy coverage was already well below the metro average.

Lower canopy levels are tied directly to higher summer heat, increased stormwater runoff, and worse air quality for the households below. Researchers and city foresters are urging faster replanting programs in the hardest-hit neighborhoods, particularly along streets where older ash trees have been removed but not replaced.

Both Minneapolis and St. Paul offer free or low-cost residential tree programs, and several nonprofits run volunteer planting days each spring and fall.
bodyMedium: A new analysis finds the Twin Cities metro lost 8% of urban tree canopy since 2015. The steepest losses are in North Minneapolis and East St. Paul, where coverage was already low.
bodyLight: Twin Cities tree canopy down 8% since 2015, driven by emerald ash borer and drought.

title: Hennepin County Pilots Guaranteed Income Program for 200 Families
bodyHeavy:
Hennepin County announced a two-year guaranteed income pilot that will provide $500 per month to 200 families earning below 30% of area median income. Participants were selected by lottery from roughly 4,800 applicants in North Minneapolis and Brooklyn Park zip codes with the highest rates of housing instability, eviction filings, and emergency shelter use.

County officials say the program is funded by federal ARPA dollars and will be evaluated by the University of Minnesota Humphrey School, which will track outcomes including housing stability, employment, child well-being, and use of county safety-net programs. Researchers will compare outcomes to a control group of applicants who were not selected in the lottery but agreed to participate in periodic surveys.

Payments will be delivered via prepaid debit card and are structured so they do not count as income for most federal benefits. A benefits counselor will be available throughout the pilot to help families navigate interactions with SNAP, medical assistance, and housing subsidies — an issue that tripped up similar pilots in St. Paul and Minneapolis when participants lost partial benefits after receiving payments.

Payments begin next month. Earlier Hennepin County guaranteed-income experiments have focused on specific populations such as formerly homeless adults; the new pilot is the county's largest to date and its first to include families with children as the primary target group.

The county will publish preliminary outcome data after the first year. Advocates hope the results will build support for a permanent, state-funded program if the pilot shows sustained improvements in housing stability.
bodyMedium: Hennepin County will give $500/month to 200 low-income families in a two-year guaranteed income pilot. Payments begin next month, funded by federal ARPA dollars and evaluated by the U of M Humphrey School.
bodyLight: Hennepin County launches $500/month guaranteed income pilot for 200 families.

meta.deck: Two hundred families in North Minneapolis and Brooklyn Park will receive $500 per month, with outcomes tracked by the U of M Humphrey School.

title: St. Paul Library System Expands Multilingual Story Hours
bodyHeavy:
The St. Paul Public Library announced new weekly story hours in Somali, Hmong, Karen, and Spanish at four branch locations. The programs are part of a broader effort to make library resources accessible to families who speak languages other than English at home.

Each session includes read-alouds, crafts, and connections to library card sign-up in home languages. Staff worked with community partners including the Karen Organization of Minnesota and Hmong American Partnership to select books that reflect the cultural backgrounds of attending families and to recruit bilingual volunteers as co-readers.

Families do not need a library card to attend. Sessions are free, drop-in, and run weekly at the Rondo, Rice Street, Sun Ray, and East Side branches. Parents and caregivers are encouraged to come early; each branch sets aside books and crafts for families to take home.
bodyMedium: St. Paul Public Library launches weekly story hours in Somali, Hmong, Karen, and Spanish at four branches, with read-alouds, crafts, and library card sign-up in home languages.
bodyLight: St. Paul libraries add weekly story hours in four languages at four branches.

title: Dakota County Food Shelves Report 40% Surge in First-Time Visitors
bodyHeavy:
Food shelves across Dakota County saw a 40% jump in first-time visitors over the most recent two-month reporting window, according to data compiled by 360 Communities. Staff attribute the surge to rising grocery costs and the end of pandemic-era SNAP supplements.

The Open Door Pantry in Eagan and Hastings Family Service both reported they needed emergency restocking from Second Harvest Heartland to keep pace with demand. Staff at both organizations say they are seeing more working families, more seniors on fixed incomes, and more immigrant families who had never visited a food shelf before.

The food shelf network is asking for donations of shelf-stable protein, cooking oil, and culturally specific staples. Volunteers are also needed for evening and weekend shifts, when intake has been busiest.
bodyMedium: Dakota County food shelves saw 40% more first-time visitors in recent months, driven by grocery inflation and the end of pandemic SNAP supplements. Pantries are calling for restocking help.
bodyLight: Dakota County food shelves report 40% jump in first-time visitors.

meta.deck: Rising grocery costs and the end of pandemic SNAP supplements are pushing more working families to the county's pantries.

title: Inside the Push to Build 1,000 Affordable Units Along the Green Line
bodyHeavy:
A coalition of nonprofit developers and community organizations is pressing the Met Council to fund 1,000 new affordable housing units along the Green Line corridor between downtown Minneapolis and downtown St. Paul. The proposal targets vacant and underused parcels within a quarter-mile of stations, including surface parking lots owned by public agencies and several decommissioned commercial buildings along University Avenue.

Advocates say the corridor's transit access makes it ideal for car-free households, many of whom are immigrants and refugees who already rely on the Green Line for work and school. Aeon, Project for Pride in Living, and CommonBond Communities have each committed pre-development funding, contingent on Met Council participation. The coalition has identified twenty-two candidate sites and estimates construction could begin on the first phase within eighteen months of funding approval.

Opponents on some city councils worry about density and parking, particularly in Midway neighborhoods where residents have raised concerns about the pace of development. Planning staff in St. Paul have signaled that zoning changes adopted after the Green Line opened in 2014 already permit the heights proposed, but that parking minimums could require variances at several sites.

The Met Council is expected to vote in the coming weeks. If the full 1,000 units are funded, it would be the largest transit-oriented affordable housing commitment in the region since the Blue Line extension was first planned. Community organizations including HOME Line have signaled support, noting that corridor rents have risen sharply over the last three years.
bodyMedium: A nonprofit coalition is pushing the Met Council to fund 1,000 affordable units along the Green Line. Advocates cite transit access for car-free households; a vote is expected in the coming weeks.
bodyLight: Coalition pushes for 1,000 affordable units along the Green Line corridor.

meta.deck: A nonprofit coalition targets vacant parcels along the Green Line for car-free affordable housing—the largest transit-oriented commitment in years if it passes.

title: Scott County Schools See Record English Learner Enrollment
bodyHeavy:
Shakopee and Prior Lake-Savage school districts enrolled a combined 1,800 English learners this year, a record driven by families arriving from East Africa, Central America, and South Asia. District officials are hiring additional EL staff and expanding summer bridge programs to help students transition.

Both districts say the growth has stretched existing EL staffing models, and both are actively recruiting bilingual teachers. Community partners including the CAP Agency Scott County office are helping families connect with food, clothing, and benefits assistance while students settle into the school year.

Summer bridge programs, which combine intensive English instruction with orientation to district routines, have been expanded to run six weeks instead of four. Families can register through their school's main office or the district EL department.
bodyMedium: Shakopee and Prior Lake-Savage districts enrolled a record 1,800 English learners, driven by new immigrant families. Districts are hiring EL staff and expanding summer bridge programs.

title: Stillwater's First Community Fridge Opens on Main Street
bodyHeavy:
A community fridge stocked by local restaurants and gardeners opened on Main Street in Stillwater this month, the first of its kind in Washington County. Valley Outreach helped coordinate permits and volunteer schedules. Anyone can take what they need—no ID, no questions.

The fridge is restocked daily by a rotating team of neighbors and by three downtown restaurants that set aside prepared meals that would otherwise be discarded. A second, smaller shelf next to the fridge holds shelf-stable pantry items and toiletries for people who need them but do not have refrigeration at home.

Organizers are recruiting additional volunteers to cover weekend cleaning and restocking shifts. Donations of produce, eggs, dairy, and ready-to-eat meals are welcome; organizers ask that all donated food be clearly labeled with a date.
bodyMedium: Stillwater's first community fridge opened this month on Main Street, stocked daily by three nearby restaurants and a weekend rotation of neighbors. No ID, no sign-in, no questions — take what you need when you need it.

title: Metro Transit Ridership Rebounds to Pre-Pandemic Levels on Blue Line
bodyHeavy:
Blue Line ridership hit pre-pandemic levels last month for the first time, averaging 28,000 boardings per weekday. Metro Transit credits increased service frequency and a fare-free pilot for youth under 18.

Agency planners say the recovery has been strongest at stations near community colleges and at south-end stations serving the Mall of America employment cluster. Weekend ridership has surpassed the pre-pandemic baseline for three months running, and bicycle boardings — tracked separately — are at record highs.

Metro Transit plans to maintain current frequencies through the summer and will evaluate whether to extend the youth fare-free program after the legislative session.
bodyMedium: Blue Line ridership returned to pre-pandemic levels last month, averaging 28,000 daily boardings thanks to increased frequency and free youth fares under 18.
bodyLight: Blue Line ridership back to pre-pandemic levels on increased frequency and free youth fares.

title: New Law Expands Tenant Protections for Families With Children
bodyHeavy:
A new Minnesota law taking effect next month prohibits landlords from advertising units as "adults only" or denying applications based on the presence of children under 18. The law also caps application fees at $50 and requires landlords to provide a written reason for denial.

Housing advocates say the measure will particularly benefit immigrant families, who disproportionately face familial-status discrimination. HOME Line, the state's tenant hotline, has been preparing a multilingual guide to help renters understand the new protections and document violations if they encounter them.

Renters who believe they have been denied based on the presence of children can file complaints with the Minnesota Department of Human Rights. Attorneys with HOME Line, the Advocates for Human Rights, and the Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota can advise on next steps, including whether to file in conciliation court.

Landlords covered by the law should update application forms and advertising language before the effective date to avoid liability.
bodyMedium: A new Minnesota law effective next month bans "adults only" rental ads, caps application fees at $50, and requires written denial reasons. Advocates say it will particularly help immigrant families.
bodyLight: New MN law expands tenant protections for families with children.

title: Washington County Opens New Mental Health Crisis Center
bodyHeavy:
Washington County opened a 16-bed mental health crisis center in Stillwater that offers walk-in services and short-term stabilization as an alternative to emergency rooms. The center operates 24/7 and accepts all residents regardless of insurance status.

Staff include licensed counselors and peer support specialists who have lived experience with mental health challenges. Typical stays range from a few hours to three days, with follow-up appointments scheduled before discharge. County officials say the goal is to reduce emergency-room boarding, which has been the most common path to care for people in crisis.

The center accepts walk-ins at all hours and can dispatch a mobile team when callers cannot travel. County crisis lines and 988 will route calls to the center when appropriate.
bodyMedium: Washington County opened a 24/7 walk-in mental health crisis center in Stillwater with 16 beds, offering stabilization services regardless of insurance status.
bodyLight: Washington County opens 24/7 mental health crisis center in Stillwater.

meta.deck: Walk-in crisis center offers an alternative to the ER, with peer support specialists and no insurance requirement.

title: Brooklyn Park Youth Program Graduates First Cohort of Apprentices
bodyHeavy:
A youth workforce program in Brooklyn Park graduated its first cohort of 24 apprentices, who completed paid internships in healthcare, IT, and skilled trades. The program, a partnership between Hennepin Technical College and local employers, targets teens from immigrant families.

Apprentices earned roughly $18 per hour during their placements and will continue with hiring employers or enter college pathways. Program staff say the retention rate after the first six months has exceeded 80%, and a second cohort is already enrolled.

Community partners including CAPI USA helped with outreach and supported families through the application process.
bodyMedium: A Brooklyn Park youth program graduated 24 apprentices in healthcare, IT, and trades—a partnership between Hennepin Tech and local employers targeting immigrant teens.

title: Eagan Breaks Ground on Affordable Senior Housing Complex
bodyHeavy:
Construction began on a 72-unit affordable senior housing complex near the Eagan Transit Station. The development will serve residents earning 30–50% of area median income and includes ground-floor community space, on-site case management, and connections to Metro Transit.

Units should be available for lease-up in late 2027. The project team includes a nonprofit developer, the City of Eagan, and Dakota County, which contributed gap financing and will coordinate resident services with local partners.

On-site services will include benefits navigation and food-shelf delivery partnerships with The Open Door Pantry in Eagan, which already coordinates home delivery for homebound seniors in the area.

title: Ramsey County Launches Eviction Diversion Pilot
bodyHeavy:
Ramsey County launched an eviction diversion program that pairs tenants facing eviction with mediators and emergency rental assistance before a case goes to court. The pilot, funded through a state grant, aims to reduce formal eviction filings by 25% in the first year.

Participating landlords agree to a 30-day mediation window in exchange for guaranteed rent payments through the program. Tenants work with a navigator from Community Action Partnership of Ramsey & Washington Counties, who helps them apply for assistance, set up payment plans, and connect with HOME Line attorneys if needed.

County officials say eviction filings in the first months of the pilot are down meaningfully compared to the same period last year, though it is too early to attribute that fully to the program. A full evaluation is planned after the first twelve months.
bodyMedium: Ramsey County's new eviction diversion program pairs tenants with mediators and rental assistance before court. The pilot aims to cut filings by 25% in the first year.
bodyLight: Ramsey County launches eviction diversion pilot to cut filings by 25%.

title: Community Solar Garden Brings Lower Bills to 300 Renters in Brooklyn Center
bodyHeavy:
A community solar garden in Brooklyn Center is now delivering credits to roughly 300 renter households, cutting their electricity bills by an average of 15%. The project is one of the first in Minnesota specifically designed for renters, who traditionally cannot install rooftop panels.

Subscribers pay nothing up front and see the credit applied directly on their Xcel Energy bills. CEAP helped recruit the initial subscriber pool from households already connected to its benefits programs. Program staff say the savings are most meaningful for fixed-income seniors and single-parent households, for whom a 15% drop in utility costs can free up funds for groceries or transit.

A waitlist is open for additional households. Residents can check eligibility through CEAP's housing and benefits office.
bodyMedium: A Brooklyn Center community solar garden is cutting electricity bills by 15% for 300 renter households—one of the first renter-focused solar projects in Minnesota, coordinated through CEAP.
bodyLight: Brooklyn Center solar garden cuts bills for 300 renters.

title: Local Chef Turns Food Waste Into 500 Free Meals a Week
bodyHeavy:
Chef Amina Hassan recovers surplus produce from restaurants and grocers along Lake Street and transforms it into 500 free meals each week, distributed through three community centers in South Minneapolis. Her operation runs on volunteer labor and donated commercial kitchen space, with zero government funding.

Hassan says the meals are culturally appropriate—halal, vegetarian, and allergen-labeled—because she asks recipients what they actually want to eat. Menus rotate weekly and include East African, Latin American, and Southeast Asian staples. Distribution partners include CLUES Minneapolis and Pillsbury United Communities' Brian Coyle Center, both of which help identify families who would benefit from a weekly meal delivery.

Volunteers are welcome in the kitchen on weekday afternoons. Donations of produce from home gardens and business kitchens are accepted with a 24-hour heads-up, and Hassan will coordinate pickup for qualifying donations.
bodyMedium: Chef Amina Hassan turns surplus Lake Street produce into 500 free culturally appropriate meals per week, distributed through three South Minneapolis community centers in partnership with CLUES and Brian Coyle.
bodyLight: Lake Street chef turns food waste into 500 free meals a week.

meta.deck: Chef Amina Hassan recovers surplus produce and cooks 500 free meals a week for South Minneapolis—no government funding, just neighbors.

title: Winter Shelter Beds Available Tonight—Walk-ins Welcome
bodyHeavy:
Overflow shelters open tonight at Simpson Housing (2740 1st Ave S) and Mary's Place (401 N 7th St). No ID required and no appointment needed. Temperatures are forecast to drop to −8°F overnight with wind chills approaching −25°F, a level at which Hennepin County activates its cold-weather emergency protocols.

Beds are first come, first served. Doors open in the early evening and close in the morning. Guests receive a cot, blanket, access to restrooms, and a light meal. Pets are welcome at Simpson Housing; Mary's Place does not accommodate pets but will coordinate with Animal Humane Society for short-term fostering on request. Both sites have staff on hand who speak Spanish, Somali, and Oromo, and translation by phone is available in other languages.

Metro Transit routes 5 and 14 stop within one block of both shelters and will run on regular schedule overnight. Riders who cannot pay fare should board and ask the operator; Metro Transit has suspended fare enforcement during the cold-weather emergency. Call 612-874-8683 for transport assistance if you cannot reach a shelter on your own; a van from St. Stephen's Human Services can pick up callers anywhere in Hennepin County in the hours before shelter doors open.

If you know someone sleeping outside tonight, outreach teams are actively checking encampments and can be reached through the same number.
bodyMedium: Overflow shelters open tonight at Simpson Housing and Mary's Place. No ID required. Temps dropping to −8°F with wind chills near −25°F. Call 612-874-8683 for transport help.
bodyLight: Shelter beds available tonight. No ID required. Call 612-874-8683.

title: Boil Water Advisory—North Minneapolis
bodyHeavy:
A water main break at 26th and Lyndale has prompted a precautionary boil water advisory for residents between 24th and 28th Streets, from Hennepin Avenue east to Nicollet Avenue. Affected households should boil tap water for at least one minute before drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, or preparing baby formula until further notice. Water used for washing hands, showering, and laundry does not need to be boiled.

The advisory will remain in place until two consecutive rounds of bacterial testing come back clear, a process that typically takes 24 to 48 hours after repairs are complete. City crews are on site and expect pipe repairs to take 24 to 48 hours, with the testing window beginning after water service is fully restored.

Free bottled water is available at Whittier Park recreation center from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. while the advisory is active. Residents can take up to two cases per household per day; those without transportation can call 311 to request delivery from a volunteer through the Minneapolis Emergency Management neighbor-help line.

Hennepin County Public Health advises parents of infants and medically fragile residents to rely on bottled water throughout the advisory period. Updates, including the results of each testing round, will be posted at minneapolismn.gov/water and shared through the city's text alert system. Residents can sign up for alerts by texting WATER to 468311.
bodyMedium: A water main break at 26th and Lyndale has prompted a precautionary boil water advisory between 24th and 28th Streets. Boil tap water one minute before drinking, cooking, or making baby formula. Free bottled water is at Whittier Park rec center, 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., while crews complete repairs.
bodyLight: Boil water advisory covers 24th–28th Streets in North Minneapolis.

title: ICE Activity Reported Near Lake Street
bodyHeavy:
Community members reported ICE agents operating near Lake Street and Chicago Avenue earlier today. Rapid response volunteers from area legal aid groups confirmed the activity and are monitoring the corridor. Neighbors are asked to share verified reports and avoid spreading unconfirmed rumors that can cause panic.

Know your rights: you do not have to open your door to immigration officers without a judicial warrant signed by a judge, which is different from an ICE administrative warrant. You have the right to remain silent and the right to speak with a lawyer before answering questions or signing anything. Do not sign documents you do not understand.

The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota hotline at 651-641-1011 is staffed to take reports, dispatch legal observers, and connect families with attorneys. Save the number in your phone. If someone in your household is detained, call the hotline immediately so attorneys can locate them before transfer. Know-your-rights cards in multiple languages are available from ILCM and The Advocates for Human Rights.
bodyMedium: Community members report ICE agents operating near Lake Street and Chicago Avenue. You do not have to open your door without a judicial warrant signed by a judge, and you have the right to remain silent. Call the Immigrant Law Center hotline at 651-641-1011 to report activity or reach an attorney.
bodyLight: ICE activity reported near Lake Street. Know your rights. Call 651-641-1011.

title: Hennepin County Opens Three New Overnight Warming Centers
bodyHeavy:
Three new overnight warming centers have opened in Brooklyn Center, downtown Minneapolis, and Bloomington to expand shelter-of-last-resort capacity during the remaining weeks of the cold season. Each site runs from 6 p.m. to 8 a.m. nightly, offers hot beverages and light snacks, and staffs an outreach worker who can connect guests with longer-term shelter beds, housing navigation, and medical referrals.

No ID, sobriety assessment, or paperwork is required for entry. Guests can arrive any time during operating hours and may stay the full overnight window. Each site has accessible entrances, and Brooklyn Center and Bloomington have space for a limited number of pets on leashes or in carriers. The downtown site does not yet accommodate pets but keeps a list of partner shelters that do.

Transportation support is available on cold-advisory nights through the Hennepin County 211 line, which can dispatch a ride for guests who cannot safely travel to a warming center on their own. The expansion is a joint effort of Hennepin County, Community Action Partnership of Hennepin County, and the host municipalities, and runs through the end of March.
bodyMedium: Three new overnight warming centers in Brooklyn Center, downtown Minneapolis, and Bloomington are open 6 p.m. to 8 a.m. nightly through the end of the cold season. No ID is required. Outreach workers can connect guests with shelter beds and housing referrals, and 211 can dispatch rides on cold-advisory nights.
bodyLight: Three new overnight warming centers open in Hennepin County. No ID required.

title: MNsure Open Enrollment Window Extended
bodyHeavy:
Minnesotans who missed the initial open enrollment deadline still have a few weeks to apply for health coverage through MNsure, the state's health insurance marketplace. Free, trained navigators can walk applicants through plan options, subsidy eligibility, and Medical Assistance or MinnesotaCare determinations by phone or in person.

Navigators are reachable at 855-366-7873 and through MNsure's online appointment tool. In-person enrollment events are scheduled at libraries, clinics, and community centers across the metro in the coming weeks, with language assistance available in Somali, Hmong, Spanish, and Karen at most sites. Interpreters for other languages can be requested in advance.

Applicants should have their household income information, Social Security numbers for everyone on the application, and information about any job-based coverage they already have. Coverage starts the first of the month after enrollment. After the extended window closes, MNsure's year-round enrollment is limited to people with a qualifying life event such as a job loss, move, marriage, or new baby.
bodyMedium: Minnesotans who missed open enrollment can still apply for MNsure health coverage during an extended window. Free navigators at 855-366-7873 can help by phone or at in-person events across the metro, with language help in Somali, Hmong, Spanish, and Karen. Bring income and Social Security information.
bodyLight: MNsure extends open enrollment. Free navigator help at 855-366-7873.

title: Free Tax Prep Sites Open at 12 Hennepin County Libraries
bodyHeavy:
Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) volunteers are helping Hennepin County residents file federal and state returns at 12 library branches this tax season. The service is free to households earning roughly $64,000 or less per year and is staffed by IRS-certified preparers who specialize in working-family credits like the Earned Income Tax Credit and the Minnesota Working Family Credit.

Walk-ins are accepted on Saturdays from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. at each participating branch. Weekday appointments are available by calling ahead; the library system's main number and the branch schedules list current availability. Filers should bring a photo ID, Social Security cards for everyone on the return, all W-2s and 1099s, last year's return if available, and bank information if they want their refund direct deposited.

Returns are typically completed in a single 60- to 90-minute visit. Volunteers can also help residents claim the Renters' Property Tax Refund and the Minnesota K-12 Education Credit, both of which are frequently missed.
bodyMedium: VITA volunteers are helping Hennepin County residents file federal and state returns free of charge at 12 library branches. Walk-ins are welcome on Saturdays from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m., and weekday appointments are available by phone. Bring ID, Social Security cards, W-2s, and last year's return.
bodyLight: Free tax prep at 12 Hennepin County libraries. Walk-ins on Saturdays.

title: March SNAP EBT Deposits Will Be Staggered Over Ten Business Days
bodyHeavy:
March SNAP deposits to EBT cards will be released on a staggered schedule over 10 business days, the same pattern the Minnesota Department of Human Services has used in recent months. Each household's deposit date depends on the last digit of the recipient's case number, and the exact calendar is posted at mn.gov/dhs/snap.

Recipients can also check the deposit date by calling their county human services office or by logging in to the MNbenefits portal. Balances can be checked by calling the number on the back of the EBT card, by text alert, or online.

If an EBT card is lost, stolen, or damaged, the 24-hour EBT replacement line at 888-997-2227 can freeze the card immediately and mail a replacement, which typically arrives in five to seven business days. Households that experience EBT theft through card skimming may be eligible for benefit replacement; county offices can walk recipients through the claim form, and legal aid organizations can help if a claim is denied.
bodyMedium: March SNAP deposits will be staggered over 10 business days, with each household's date tied to the last digit of its case number. Check your date at mn.gov/dhs/snap or through the MNbenefits portal. If your EBT card is lost or stolen, call 888-997-2227 for an immediate freeze and replacement.
bodyLight: March SNAP deposits staggered over 10 business days. Check your date online.

title: SNAP Benefits Processing May Run Days Behind This Week
bodyHeavy:
A statewide system update is expected to slow SNAP benefit processing by two to three business days this week. Recipients whose usual deposit date falls during the update window may see their EBT balance refresh later than normal. The Department of Human Services says benefits will not be reduced by the delay; only the timing shifts.

If a deposit has not arrived within 48 hours of the expected date, recipients should first check the MNbenefits portal or call the number on the back of their EBT card for a balance. New applications and recertifications submitted this week may also take a few extra days to process; households in urgent need of food should contact their local food shelf while they wait. Hunger Solutions' food shelf locator and the 211 line can identify nearby sites with same-day pickup. If a case worker has not responded within a week of a status inquiry, call the county office directly to escalate.
bodyMedium: A statewide system update is expected to slow SNAP benefit processing by two to three business days this week. Benefits will not be reduced. If a deposit has not arrived 48 hours past the expected date, check the portal or call your county office. Food shelves can bridge the gap.
bodyLight: SNAP processing delayed 2–3 days this week. Wait 48 hours before calling.

title: Minneapolis Public Schools Moves to Remote Learning on Snow Days
bodyHeavy:
Minneapolis Public Schools is shifting its snow-day practice to use remote learning rather than canceling classes outright when roads become unsafe. The district says the change preserves instructional time and avoids adding make-up days to the end of the school year, where they conflict with summer programming and family travel.

On snow days, families will receive a text alert by 5:30 a.m. and an email confirming that classes will run remotely. Teachers post the day's lesson and assignments in the district's learning portal, and students are expected to log in for at least part of the day. Attendance on remote-learning days is counted the same as in-person attendance.

Students without a device or home internet can contact their school's family liaison to borrow a district laptop and a hotspot. Breakfast and lunch pickup will still be offered at designated school sites on remote-learning days so families who rely on school meals are not left without food. The district is also coordinating with after-school partners so that child care programs running on-site know ahead of time whether they can open.
bodyMedium: Minneapolis Public Schools will use remote learning on snow days instead of canceling classes, preserving instructional time. Families receive a text alert by 5:30 a.m. Students without a device or internet can borrow a laptop and hotspot from their school's family liaison, and meal pickup is still available.
bodyLight: MPS switches to remote learning on snow days. Text alert by 5:30 a.m.

title: Metro Transit Adds Overnight Service on Route 5 Through Chicago Avenue
bodyHeavy:
Metro Transit is adding overnight service on Route 5 along Chicago Avenue starting at the next schedule change, with buses running every 30 minutes from midnight to 5 a.m. The route connects North Minneapolis through downtown to the Mall of America, passing stops near hospital complexes, 24-hour retail employers, and the airport-area lodging corridor.

Transit planners say the overnight block is aimed at the thousands of metro workers whose shifts begin or end outside traditional bus hours, including hotel housekeepers, warehouse staff, medical aides, and airport contractors. Ridership on the neighboring overnight Route 54 has grown steadily since that line added 24-hour service, and Route 5's ridership numbers suggested a similar unmet need.

Fares during overnight hours match the standard local fare. Real-time arrival information is available in the Metro Transit app and at signed stops. The service is being launched as a year-long pilot; transit officials will evaluate ridership and on-time performance before deciding whether to make the schedule permanent.
bodyMedium: Metro Transit is adding overnight service on Route 5 along Chicago Avenue, with buses every 30 minutes from midnight to 5 a.m. The route connects North Minneapolis through downtown to the Mall of America, serving hospital, warehouse, and airport-area shift workers. It launches as a year-long pilot.
bodyLight: Route 5 adds overnight service every 30 minutes, midnight to 5 a.m.

title: Call Your Legislator: Fund Minnesota's Emergency Rental Assistance
bodyHeavy:
HF 1247 would allocate $50 million to refill Minnesota's emergency rental assistance fund, which tenant advocates say is on track to run out before the end of the summer. The bill is currently in committee, which is the window when a single phone call from a constituent carries the most weight — committee members decide whether the bill moves to the floor at all.

HOME Line, Minnesota's free tenant hotline, is asking renters, landlords, and community members to call their state representative and senator and say they support HF 1247. Callers can keep the message short: their name, their city or ZIP, and a one-sentence ask to vote yes on HF 1247 to fund emergency rental assistance.

The legislator-finder tool at the Legislative Coordinating Commission maps any Minnesota address to the right House and Senate offices. HOME Line's hotline can also look up a constituent's legislators and coach callers through the ask, in English, Spanish, Somali, or Hmong. Calls placed during business hours are logged by staff; messages left after hours are counted the next morning.
bodyMedium: HF 1247 would allocate $50 million to refill Minnesota's emergency rental assistance fund, which advocates say will run out by summer. The bill is in committee — the point in the process when a constituent call carries the most weight. HOME Line's hotline can look up your legislators and coach you through the ask.
bodyLight: Call your legislator: vote yes on HF 1247 to fund rental assistance.

title: Make Your Community Count in the Census
bodyHeavy:
Every person counted in the census translates into federal dollars for Minnesota schools, road projects, emergency services, and health clinics — dollars that follow the count for the next decade. A single household missed means thousands of dollars in funding that goes elsewhere.

Self-response is available online, by phone, or by mail. Anyone without internet at home can complete the form at one of the assistance centers set up at libraries, community centers, and churches across the metro. The centers keep private, walk-up terminals and have trained helpers who can read the questions aloud, translate the answers, or complete the form on behalf of someone who is more comfortable speaking than typing.

Language support is available in 12 languages, including Spanish, Somali, Hmong, Karen, Amharic, and Vietnamese. Census responses are confidential by federal law and cannot be shared with landlords, immigration authorities, or other government agencies. The form asks about people who live in the household, not their immigration status.
bodyMedium: Every person counted means more federal funding for Minnesota schools, roads, emergency services, and clinics over the next decade. Self-response is available online, by phone, or by mail. Assistance centers at libraries and community spaces offer private help in 12 languages.
bodyLight: Every person counted means more funding for Minnesota. Self-respond today.

title: Register to Vote or Check Your Registration Status
bodyHeavy:
Minnesota is one of a handful of states that allows voters to register at the polls on Election Day, but registering ahead of time saves a long wait at the check-in table and keeps lines moving for everyone else. Registration is free, takes about five minutes online, and updates automatically when you move within the state.

To register or check your current status, visit mnvotes.org. First-time voters and people who have moved need a valid ID and proof of residence, such as a recent utility bill, bank statement, lease, or piece of government mail with the current address. New citizens can use their naturalization certificate as proof of eligibility, and students can use a student ID plus a dorm list from their school. Tribal ID cards are also accepted statewide.

If a voter already registered in Minnesota has moved, voted under a different name, or hasn't voted in four or more years, their record may need to be refreshed. The online portal flags records that need an update and walks the voter through the short form in English, Spanish, Somali, and Hmong.
bodyMedium: Minnesota allows Election Day registration, but registering early saves time at the polls. Check your status at mnvotes.org. Bring a valid ID and proof of residence, like a utility bill, lease, bank statement, or government mail. The portal flags records that need an update.
bodyLight: Register to vote or check your status at mnvotes.org.

title: Ramsey County Emergency Assistance Moves Online
bodyHeavy:
Ramsey County residents can now start an emergency assistance application online at ramseycountymn.gov. The portal covers rent and utility crisis funds, back-rent pay-downs that prevent eviction, and other one-time crisis needs such as moving expenses or security deposits for a new unit. Paper applications are still accepted in person at the Northern Service Center in West St. Paul.

The online form asks for basic household information, proof of the crisis (an eviction notice, shut-off notice, or lease), and documentation of income for everyone in the household. The county's guidance lists every document the caseworker will need before issuing a decision, so applicants can gather paperwork once rather than making multiple trips.

Phone support is available in English, Somali, Hmong, Spanish, and Karen at 651-266-4884. Staff can walk applicants through the online form, accept documents by email or fax, and schedule an in-person appointment when the situation is too complex for the portal alone. A new triage line inside the main number routes calls with immediate shut-off or lockout risk to a same-day caseworker.
bodyMedium: Ramsey County residents can now start emergency assistance applications online at ramseycountymn.gov. The portal covers rent, utilities, and related crisis needs. Paper applications are still accepted at the Northern Service Center. Phone help is available in five languages at 651-266-4884.
bodyLight: Ramsey County emergency assistance moves online. Help line: 651-266-4884.

title: Severe Thunderstorm Watch Issued for the Twin Cities Metro
bodyHeavy:
The National Weather Service has issued a severe thunderstorm watch for the Twin Cities metro through late evening tonight. Forecasters say large hail and damaging straight-line winds are the primary hazards, with isolated tornadoes possible if the atmosphere destabilizes faster than current models project.

Residents should secure or bring inside loose outdoor items — patio furniture, garbage cans, kids' toys, and anything else the wind could turn into a projectile. Vehicles parked on the street should move under cover when possible. During the storm, stay indoors, away from windows, and on the lowest floor of the building. If a tornado warning is issued, move to an interior room or basement.

City and county warming centers and overnight shelters will stay open through the storm for anyone without safe shelter. Public transit may run on delay if lightning forces buses to hold. Residents can monitor conditions on 511mn.org, the National Weather Service mobile app, and local TV and radio. Power outage maps for Xcel Energy and CenterPoint are linked from the state emergency management site.
bodyMedium: The National Weather Service has issued a severe thunderstorm watch for the Twin Cities metro through late evening. Large hail and damaging winds are expected, with isolated tornadoes possible. Secure outdoor items, stay indoors on the lowest floor, and monitor local media. Shelters remain open.
bodyLight: Severe thunderstorm watch for the Twin Cities metro. Stay indoors.

title: Dakota County WIC Offices Extend Hours for Working Parents
bodyHeavy:
Dakota County's WIC offices in Burnsville and Apple Valley have added evening and Saturday hours aimed at parents who cannot easily take time off during a standard workday. The program provides nutrition counseling, breastfeeding support, and food benefits to pregnant women, new mothers, infants, and children under age five who meet income guidelines.

Tuesday and Thursday appointments now run until 7 p.m., and Saturday walk-ins are available from 9 a.m. to noon. Applicants should bring a photo ID, proof of Minnesota residence such as a utility bill or lease, proof of income for the past month, and the children who will be on the case for a quick height and weight check.

WIC benefits are loaded onto an eWIC card that works at most grocery stores and some farmers markets. Staff say the extended hours came directly out of a parent advisory group that said traditional office hours were the single biggest barrier to staying enrolled. Appointments can be scheduled by phone or through the county's online form, and interpreters are available by request.
bodyMedium: Dakota County's WIC offices in Burnsville and Apple Valley have added Tuesday and Thursday evening appointments until 7 p.m. and Saturday walk-ins from 9 a.m. to noon. Parents should bring ID, proof of residence, and proof of income. The extended hours target families who can't visit during a standard workday.
bodyLight: Dakota County WIC extends hours in Burnsville and Apple Valley.

title: Free School Supply Kits at Three North Minneapolis Libraries
bodyHeavy:
Free spring-semester school supply kits are available — while supplies last — at North Regional, Webber Park, and Sumner libraries. Each kit includes a pack of spiral notebooks, a set of pencils and erasers, folders in a range of colors, a ruler, and a basic calculator for math-track students. Staff pulled the kit contents from back-to-school supply lists circulated by nearby elementary and middle schools so the items match what teachers are actually asking for.

The program is open to any K–12 student living in Hennepin County. No ID, library card, or proof of enrollment is required, and kits can be picked up by a parent or caregiver on the student's behalf. Families with multiple students can take one kit per child; the limit exists only so supplies stretch to the last families through the end of the semester.

Libraries are also keeping a smaller bag of replacement supplies on hand for students who run out mid-semester. The program is stocked by donations from Hennepin County Library's Friends group and several area workplaces; any remaining surplus at the end of the semester will be rolled into the summer learning programs.
bodyMedium: Free spring school supply kits are available at North Regional, Webber Park, and Sumner libraries while supplies last. Each kit includes notebooks, pencils, folders, a ruler, and a calculator pulled from local school supply lists. One kit per student, K–12; no ID or library card required.
bodyLight: Free school supply kits at three North Minneapolis libraries.

title: Shakopee Food Shelf Adds Halal and Latino Staples
bodyHeavy:
CAP Agency's Shakopee food shelf has expanded its inventory to include halal meats, injera flour, masa, dried chilies, fish sauce, and other culturally specific staples that previously required a trip to a specialty grocer across the river. The change follows more than a year of feedback from Somali and Latino families, who make up a growing share of the pantry's visitors.

Staff say the decision to stock the items came out of a simple count: when volunteers started tracking what people were asking for but leaving without, culturally specific staples were the single most common gap. Partnerships with Isuroon in Burnsville and with a halal wholesale distributor made it possible to buy in volume without pushing other items off the shelves.

The Shakopee pantry is open for walk-in shopping four days a week and continues to host a once-a-month mobile distribution in Belle Plaine. Anyone who lives in Scott County can shop regardless of income, and no ID is required. Volunteers who speak Somali, Spanish, and Karen are on site during the busiest hours, and families can request a translator in advance.
bodyMedium: CAP Agency's Shakopee food shelf now stocks halal meats, injera flour, masa, dried chilies, and other culturally specific staples. The change followed year-long feedback from Somali and Latino families. Shopping is open to any Scott County resident; no ID required. Volunteers speaking Somali, Spanish, and Karen are on site.
bodyLight: Shakopee food shelf adds halal meats, injera flour, masa, and more.

title: Cottage Grove Opens Renovated Accessible Playground at Kingston Park
bodyHeavy:
Cottage Grove held a ribbon-cutting for the renovated Kingston Park playground, which now includes wheelchair-accessible surfacing and ramps, an expanded sensory play area with musical and tactile panels, and new shade structures over the climbing equipment. The project replaces a wood-chip playground that had been in place since the early 1990s and had grown difficult to navigate for kids using mobility aids.

The $1.2 million renovation was funded primarily by a state bonding allocation, with the remainder coming from the city's parks levy and community donations. A city parks planner said the accessibility upgrades were designed with input from parents in the district's special education program and from an occupational therapist who works with several Cottage Grove families.

Kingston Park will remain free and open to the public year-round; the city has committed to plowing the access paths so the playground can be used through the winter. The parks department is using lessons learned at Kingston as a template for upcoming renovations at two smaller neighborhood playgrounds scheduled for the coming year.
bodyMedium: Cottage Grove has reopened Kingston Park's renovated playground, which now features wheelchair-accessible surfacing, an expanded sensory play area, and new shade structures. The $1.2 million project was funded mainly by a state bonding allocation. Access paths will be plowed so the playground can be used year-round.
bodyLight: Cottage Grove opens renovated accessible playground at Kingston Park.

sourceAttribution.attribution: City of Cottage Grove press release

title: Free Immigration Legal Clinic This Saturday at CLUES St. Paul
bodyHeavy:
CLUES' St. Paul office is hosting a free immigration legal clinic this weekend, staffed by volunteer attorneys who specialize in DACA renewals, Temporary Protected Status applications, and family-based petitions. The clinic runs for four hours and is open to walk-ins, though appointments are preferred because they give attorneys time to review documents before the meeting.

Each appointment pairs a family with an attorney and, when needed, a Spanish-English interpreter. Staff are also on hand to help with form-filling, fee-waiver requests, and document copying. Families should bring every immigration document they have — prior filings, receipt notices, ID cards from home countries, school records for kids — even if they are unsure whether a particular paper is relevant.

Childcare is provided on site so parents can focus on the legal conversation. Clients with active removal cases should say so at check-in; those cases are routed to attorneys trained in removal defense rather than the general clinic line. The clinic is a joint program of CLUES and area immigration nonprofits; appointments can be booked by calling 651-379-4200.
bodyMedium: CLUES St. Paul is hosting a free immigration legal clinic this weekend with volunteer attorneys handling DACA renewals, TPS applications, and family-based petitions. Spanish-English interpreters and childcare are provided. Walk-ins welcome, but appointments are preferred — call 651-379-4200.
bodyLight: Free immigration legal clinic this Saturday at CLUES St. Paul.

title: Hennepin County Expands Free Diaper Program to Four Sites
bodyHeavy:
Hennepin County's free diaper distribution program, which had run for the past two years out of VEAP in Bloomington, is expanding to three additional sites across the county: CEAP in Brooklyn Center, STEP in St. Louis Park, and Sabathani Community Center in South Minneapolis. The expansion adds capacity in corners of the county that saw long waits and repeat trips across town.

Families can receive up to 50 diapers per child per month. Staff will ask for the child's approximate age and size but do not require income verification, enrollment in another program, or a referral. Parents can walk in during posted pantry hours; each site also accepts a standing monthly pickup for families who prefer not to re-register each time.

The program also distributes pull-ups for potty-training children and a small stock of adult incontinence supplies for caregivers who need them. Organizers say the goal is to cover a real gap in the family safety net: SNAP, WIC, and most emergency assistance programs cannot be spent on diapers, which are frequently the most expensive line item in a family's monthly budget.
bodyMedium: Hennepin County's free diaper program is expanding from VEAP in Bloomington to three more sites: CEAP in Brooklyn Center, STEP in St. Louis Park, and Sabathani in South Minneapolis. Families can receive up to 50 diapers per child per month with no income verification. Pull-ups and adult supplies are also distributed.
bodyLight: Free diaper program expands to four Hennepin County sites.

title: New D Line Bus Rapid Transit Station Opens at Brooklyn Boulevard
bodyHeavy:
The Brooklyn Boulevard station on Metro Transit's D Line bus rapid transit corridor opened this week, adding a heated shelter, real-time arrival displays, and level boarding for riders using wheelchairs, walkers, or strollers. The station sits at one of the busiest transfer points in the northwest metro, connecting the D Line spine to cross-town routes that serve Brooklyn Park, Maple Grove, and downtown Minneapolis.

Level boarding — where the bus floor lines up with the platform — eliminates the lift cycle that slows boarding at older stops and has been the single biggest accessibility complaint on the corridor for years. The new station also has wider sidewalks leading to the platform, a bike rack and Nice Ride dock, and emergency call buttons tied directly to Metro Transit police dispatch.

Off-board fare payment, which moved to the D Line at launch, continues at the Brooklyn Boulevard station: riders tap or insert their fare at the platform machine before the bus arrives. Route 5, which shares part of the corridor, will continue to stop at nearby local-route stops rather than the BRT platform.
bodyMedium: The Brooklyn Boulevard D Line station is now open, with a heated shelter, real-time arrival displays, and level boarding for riders using wheelchairs or strollers. It anchors a major transfer point in the northwest metro. Off-board fare payment continues; Route 5 stops at nearby local platforms.
bodyLight: New D Line BRT station open at Brooklyn Boulevard.

title: Family of Four Needs Temporary Housing in the South Metro
bodyHeavy:
A family of four — two parents with children ages three and seven — is currently staying at People Serving People's family shelter and is actively searching for a two-bedroom rental in the south metro. The parents are both employed, with combined take-home income that supports rent up to about $1,200 per month. They have completed the shelter's housing-readiness program, have rental references, and can pass a standard background and credit screening.

The family's case manager is available to landlords to coordinate a walk-through, answer questions about the family's situation, and help with the first move-in steps. Where landlords are willing, the case manager can also connect to rapid rehousing funds that cover the security deposit and first month's rent, plus limited utility startup costs.

Preferred areas are Bloomington, Richfield, and south Minneapolis, with access to a bus line for the parents' commutes and within a reasonable school-transfer distance for the seven-year-old. The family is open to a 12-month lease and is ready to move in on short notice once a unit is confirmed. Landlords, property managers, or neighbors with leads can reach the case manager through People Serving People's main line.
bodyMedium: A family of four currently in the People Serving People family shelter is looking for a 2BR rental up to $1,200 per month in Bloomington, Richfield, or south Minneapolis. Both parents work, references are available, and their case manager can coordinate move-in and connect to rapid rehousing funds.
bodyLight: Family of four needs 2BR in south metro, up to $1,200. Case manager available.

bodyLight: CEAP needs weekday-morning food shelf volunteers in Brooklyn Center. Training provided.

No experience needed — training happens on your first shift. A background check is required, and volunteers commit to one morning per week for at least three months.
bodyHeavy:
CEAP's Brooklyn Center food shelf is looking for weekday-morning volunteers to keep pace with rising demand across the northwest metro. Shifts run Tuesday through Friday from 8:30 a.m. to noon. Volunteers sort and stock donations, greet clients at intake, and help with paperwork in English, Spanish, and Somali when possible.

No prior food shelf experience is needed. Training happens on your first shift, and staff pair new volunteers with an experienced shelf lead. A background check is required before your first solo shift, and CEAP asks for a commitment of one morning per week for at least three months so regulars can build trust with the clients they see.

Rides and bus passes are available on request. Students and retirees are both strongly represented on the volunteer rotation, and CEAP will write service-hour letters for school or court requirements. Apply online or call the volunteer line to get onto the next orientation list.

bodyLight: People Serving People is accepting clean winter coats and boots weekday drop-off.
bodyMedium: People Serving People in downtown Minneapolis is accepting clean, gently used winter coats, boots, gloves, and hats for the families it shelters. Children's sizes are especially short. Drop-off runs weekday mornings and afternoons at the front desk, and donations move directly to families in shelter and transitional housing.
bodyHeavy:
People Serving People, the largest family shelter in Minnesota, is asking neighbors to clear out their coat closets. Clean, gently used winter coats, boots, gloves, and hats are needed for the roughly 200 children the shelter houses on any given night. Children's sizes are running especially thin, and new or lightly used snow pants are prioritized.

Drop-offs are accepted Monday through Friday from 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. at the front desk, 614 S 3rd Street in downtown Minneapolis. Staff can meet donors at the curb for larger loads — call ahead to set that up. New items are also welcome, and the shelter keeps a running wish list on its website for donors who want to shop rather than clean out.

Donations go directly to families living in shelter and transitional housing, with surplus shared with partner agencies across Hennepin County. Tax receipts are provided on request at drop-off.

title: ESL Conversation Partners — One Hour a Week with Adult Learners
organizationName: CAPI USA (Communities Advancing Prosperity for Immigrants)
bodyLight: CAPI matches ESL conversation partners with adult learners for one hour a week.
bodyMedium: CAPI USA in Brooklyn Center is recruiting English-conversation partners for adult immigrant and refugee learners. Volunteers meet with a matched learner for one hour a week, daytime or evening. No teaching experience is needed — CAPI provides orientation, lesson prompts, and ongoing support.
bodyHeavy:
CAPI USA pairs volunteer conversation partners with adult immigrants and refugees who want more practice speaking English outside of class. The commitment is one hour per week for at least three months, with daytime and evening meeting slots available. Sessions can happen in person at CAPI's Brooklyn Boulevard offices, at a public library, or over video call once a learner is matched.

No teaching experience is needed. CAPI provides a short orientation, ready-to-use conversation prompts, and a staff coordinator you can text with questions. The program is most helpful for learners who already attend a formal ESL class and want low-pressure practice; patience and a willingness to listen matter more than any language credential.

Bilingual volunteers — particularly Hmong, Spanish, Somali, Oromo, and Karen speakers — are especially welcome, though monolingual English speakers are also needed. Volunteers go through a short background check before their first match.

bodyLight: HAP needs Somali- and Hmong-speaking interpreters for benefits intake. Stipend available.
bodyMedium: Hmong American Partnership in St. Paul is recruiting Somali- and Hmong-speaking interpreters to help community members apply for public benefits and complete service intake. Hours are flexible, and a stipend is available for trained interpreters. HAP provides orientation and ongoing support.
bodyHeavy:
Hmong American Partnership is building its roster of Somali- and Hmong-speaking interpreters to help community members navigate the intake paperwork behind benefits like SNAP, MFIP, energy assistance, and rental help. Interpreters sit alongside a staff navigator and translate both directions — the questions on the form and the client's answers — so applicants leave with a correctly completed packet.

Hours are flexible, with most shifts running in the late morning or early afternoon at HAP's University Avenue offices. A modest stipend is available for interpreters who complete the orientation and commit to a regular shift. Interpreters do not need professional certification, but a background in health or social services is helpful and HAP can sponsor interested volunteers for further credentialing.

The need is ongoing. Bilingual college students and recent retirees make up the bulk of the current rotation, and HAP will write service letters for anyone who needs them. Orientation cohorts run every few weeks.

items.detail: HAP can sponsor further credentialing for interested volunteers.

title: Mobile Pantry Drivers — Saturdays Across Dakota County
bodyLight: The Open Door Pantry needs Saturday drivers to deliver food across Dakota County.
bodyMedium: The Open Door Pantry in Eagan is recruiting Saturday drivers to deliver food to mobile distribution sites across Dakota County. Shifts run 2 to 3 hours. A valid driver's license and the ability to lift 40 pounds are required. Mileage is reimbursed at the federal rate, and routes change weekly based on community need.
bodyHeavy:
The Open Door Pantry's Saturday mobile routes deliver groceries to neighborhoods across Dakota County where getting to a brick-and-mortar food shelf is hard — rural corners of the county, apartment complexes with limited transit, and senior housing sites that coordinate bulk pickups. The pantry needs a dependable rotation of Saturday drivers to keep those routes running.

Shifts are 2 to 3 hours long, starting mid-morning at the Eagan warehouse. Drivers pick up a pre-loaded route sheet, load their vehicle, and hand off at each site. A valid driver's license and the ability to lift 40 pounds are required. Mileage is reimbursed at the federal rate on a monthly check, and the pantry provides a cooler, hand truck, and gas card on request.

Routes change weekly based on where food is needed most, so drivers should be comfortable following a new sheet each week. Volunteers can sign up for as many or as few Saturdays as they like, and the pantry coordinator confirms assignments by the Wednesday before.

items.name: Loader/runner
items.detail: Help load route vehicles at the Eagan warehouse.

contacts.label: Warehouse check-in

title: Room Available in North Minneapolis — Low-Cost Shared House Near Bus Lines
bodyLight: Furnished room in a shared North Minneapolis house, $400/month utilities included.
bodyMedium: A reader is offering a furnished room in a shared North Minneapolis house at $400 per month with utilities included. Near bus lines 5 and 22. The household prefers someone in recovery or recently rehoused. References required, and the kitchen and bathroom are shared with a small, quiet group of roommates.
bodyHeavy:
A Root Editorial reader is offering a furnished room in a shared North Minneapolis house at $400 per month, utilities included. The house sits near West Broadway, a short walk from bus lines 5 and 22, with grocery, pharmacy, and a free clinic within half a mile.

The current household is small and quiet — three adults, no pets, no smoking indoors. The room comes with a bed, dresser, and desk; kitchen and bathroom are shared. The housemates specifically hope to welcome someone who is in active recovery or who has recently been rehoused after a period of homelessness, and they are willing to be flexible on deposit and move-in timing for the right match. References from a case manager, recovery sponsor, or past landlord are required.

The room is available immediately. Because this is a reader-submitted listing, Root Editorial has not independently verified the landlord. Prospective tenants should meet in person before exchanging any money and bring a friend or case manager to the showing.

items.name: Shared kitchen and bath
items.detail: Three-adult household; no pets, no indoor smoking.
