title: Free Furniture by Referral — Beds, Tables, and Kitchen Supplies
bodyLight: Free furniture for families setting up new homes. Case-manager referral required.
bodyMedium: VEAP in Bloomington distributes gently used furniture — beds, dressers, tables, chairs, lamps, and kitchen supplies — to households setting up a new home. Items are available by referral from a case manager. Delivery is available within Hennepin County for a modest suggested donation, waived on hardship.
bodyHeavy:
VEAP's furniture program pairs households moving into new housing — often out of shelter, transitional housing, or after a domestic-violence relocation — with the basic furniture needed to sleep, eat, and cook at home. Inventory rotates but typically includes beds, dressers, tables, chairs, lamps, couches, and kitchen starter kits.

Households come in through a case manager. VEAP works with referral partners across Hennepin County, including Simpson Housing, Catholic Charities, and county housing stability teams. Referrals include a short needs list so families can skip straight to loading day without hunting through the warehouse.

Delivery is available within Hennepin County for a modest suggested donation; the fee is waived on hardship. Pickup is also available from VEAP's Bloomington site during weekday loading hours, and volunteers are on hand to help load. Donated items are inspected and cleaned before distribution.

items.name: Beds and bed frames
items.detail: Twin, full, and queen. Mattresses are always new.

items.name: Kitchen starter kit
items.detail: Plates, utensils, pots, and basic small appliances.

title: Pro Bono Legal Help for Immigration Cases — DACA, Asylum, Family Petitions
organizationName: The Advocates for Human Rights (Immigrant Client Line)
bodyLight: Pro bono immigration attorneys available for DACA renewals, asylum, and family petitions.
bodyMedium: The Advocates for Human Rights matches eligible immigrants in the Twin Cities with pro bono attorneys for DACA renewals, asylum applications, and family petitions. Cases are placed by appointment through an intake line, and interpretation is available. There is no fee for eligible clients.
bodyHeavy:
The Advocates for Human Rights runs one of the region's largest pro bono immigration legal programs, matching eligible clients with volunteer attorneys who handle the case from intake to hearing. Case types include DACA renewals, affirmative asylum applications, family-based petitions, U visas, and relief for trafficking survivors. There is no fee for clients who qualify.

Clients start with a phone intake through The Advocates' client line. Intake staff ask about the situation, timeline, and documents on hand, then place eligible cases with a matching attorney from the pro bono panel. Interpretation is available during intake in Spanish, Somali, Hmong, Oromo, and other languages; the organization can arrange additional languages as needed.

Because demand outpaces capacity, intake slots fill quickly when policy shifts create renewal surges. Callers who cannot get through are encouraged to try again the next business day rather than leave a message. The Advocates also maintains a Know-Your-Rights library with printable materials in several languages.

sourceAttribution.sourceName: The Advocates for Human Rights (Immigrant Client Line)

items.name: DACA renewal
items.detail: Assistance with application, evidence, and fee waivers where available.

items.name: Family-based petitions
items.detail: Assistance with I-130 and related filings.

title: Free After-School Program for Ages 6–12 in North Minneapolis
organizationName: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)
bodyLight: Free after-school program for kids 6–12 runs weekday afternoons in North Minneapolis.
bodyMedium: Pillsbury United Communities runs a free after-school program for kids ages 6 to 12 in North Minneapolis. Homework help, arts, and recreation happen Monday through Friday from 3 to 6 p.m. A snack is included. Registration is open for the spring semester, and transportation is available from select North Minneapolis schools.
bodyHeavy:
Pillsbury United Communities' after-school program gives North Minneapolis families a safe, free place for kids ages 6 to 12 in the hours between the school bell and the end of the workday. Each afternoon runs on a simple rhythm: homework help, a snack, a rotating arts or enrichment block, and open recreation.

The program runs Monday through Friday from 3 to 6 p.m. A nutritious snack is included, and staff are background-checked and CPR-certified. Transportation is available from a handful of partner schools in North Minneapolis; families should ask at registration whether their school is on the current route. Spring-semester registration is open, and the program runs through early summer.

Families can register at the Brian Coyle Center or online through Pillsbury United. Scholarships are automatic for enrolled families, and the program coordinator can connect families to related supports — summer programming, food shelf access, and utility help — during the intake conversation.

sourceAttribution.sourceName: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)

items.name: Homework help
items.detail: Staff and tutors support K–6 assignments.

items.name: Arts and enrichment
items.detail: Rotating blocks — visual art, music, STEM.

title: Free Rides to Medical Appointments for Hennepin County Residents
bodyLight: Free door-to-door medical rides for eligible Hennepin County residents. Call to book.
bodyMedium: Eligible Hennepin County residents can schedule free door-to-door transportation to medical appointments. Book one to three days ahead. Wheelchair-accessible vehicles are available. Call 651-602-1111 to register and reserve rides.
bodyHeavy:
Eligible Hennepin County residents can schedule free door-to-door transportation to medical appointments through the regional Metro Mobility partner network. The service is designed for older adults and residents with disabilities who cannot use fixed-route buses, and it covers medical, dental, and behavioral health trips.

Residents book one to three days in advance. A scheduler confirms pickup address, destination, and return time, and notes any mobility aids — canes, walkers, or wheelchairs — that will travel with the rider. Wheelchair-accessible vehicles are available and must be requested at booking.

Registration is a one-time step: riders confirm eligibility, emergency contacts, and any standing appointment patterns they want on file (dialysis, chemotherapy, weekly therapy). Once registered, future rides can be booked by phone. Escorts ride free, and the service also supports Medical Assistance-covered trips.

items.name: Standing appointment
items.detail: Dialysis, chemo, weekly therapy can be set up once.

title: Free Bicycle Repair — Saturdays at Freewheel Community Workshop
bodyLight: Free Saturday bike repairs at Freewheel Community Workshop. All skill levels welcome.
bodyMedium: Bring your bike to the Freewheel Community Workshop in south Minneapolis on Saturday mornings for free repairs. Volunteers help you fix your own bike using donated parts and tools. All skill levels are welcome. Kids under 16 must have an adult present.
bodyHeavy:
The Freewheel Community Workshop in south Minneapolis runs a drop-in repair session every Saturday where neighbors can fix their own bikes with volunteer help and shop tools. The emphasis is on teaching rather than repairing: volunteers walk you through each step so you leave knowing how to do the same fix yourself next time.

Saturdays run from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Common fixes — flat tires, tune-ups, brake adjustments, basic drivetrain work — can usually be wrapped up in a single session. Donated parts are available on a suggested-donation basis, and the shop keeps a stock of common consumables (tubes, cables, brake pads) on hand.

All skill levels are welcome. Kids under 16 must have an adult present. The shop is wheelchair-accessible; bikes that need more than the drop-in window should ask about the longer-term loaner-shop program, which lets riders return for multiple sessions until the bike is road-ready.

items.name: Donated parts
items.detail: Suggested donation; tubes, cables, brake pads in stock.

items.name: Loaner-shop program
items.detail: Longer-term return visits for bigger fixes.

title: Free Sewing Machine and Fabric — Pickup in East St. Paul
bodyLight: Working Singer sewing machine and fabric, free to pick up in East St. Paul.
bodyMedium: A reader is offering a working Singer sewing machine with carrying case, plus assorted fabric and thread, free to anyone who can pick up in East St. Paul. A good starter kit for someone learning to sew or building out a small tailoring business. First respondent gets it.
bodyHeavy:
A Root Editorial reader is clearing out a sewing corner and offering the whole kit free to anyone who can haul it out of an East St. Paul apartment. The centerpiece is a working Singer sewing machine with its original carrying case, plus the foot pedal and a small accessories kit. The reader also has several bins of assorted fabric — cotton, denim, fleece scraps — and a spool rack of thread in a range of colors.

The machine has been used for hem repairs and small household projects, and the donor says it runs smoothly. No alterations guide or manual, but Singer posts manuals for older models on its website.

Pickup is from an East St. Paul apartment building with street parking; the donor can help carry the machine to a car but cannot deliver. First respondent gets it. Root Editorial has not independently verified the listing; arrange the pickup in a public-facing lobby and bring a friend if possible.

title: Somali Women's Support Circle — Weekly Peer Group at Brian Coyle Center
organizationName: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)
bodyLight: Free weekly peer support group for Somali women at Brian Coyle Center.
bodyMedium: A weekly peer support circle for Somali women meets at the Brian Coyle Center in Minneapolis on Wednesday mornings. Facilitated in Somali by a trained peer counselor, it's a place to talk about stress, isolation, and adjustment challenges. Childcare and tea are provided. No registration or referral is needed.
bodyHeavy:
Pillsbury United Communities' Brian Coyle Center hosts a weekly peer support circle for Somali women who want a quiet, confidential space to talk about stress, isolation, caregiving load, and adjustment. The group is facilitated in Somali by a trained peer counselor who can refer participants to clinical care if they want it, but the circle itself is peer support — not therapy — and no diagnosis is needed to attend.

The group meets Wednesdays from 10 a.m. to noon in the Coyle Center's second-floor community room. Childcare is provided on-site, and tea and small snacks are served throughout the session. Participants come and go as schedules allow; many attend for a few weeks during a hard season and drop in again later.

There's no registration or referral needed. Newcomers can simply walk in. Pillsbury United can also arrange a brief one-on-one with the facilitator before the first session if a participant wants to know what to expect.

sourceAttribution.sourceName: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)

title: Volunteer Tax Preparers Needed — VITA Program at Twin Cities Libraries
bodyLight: VITA needs bilingual volunteer tax preparers across Hennepin and Ramsey counties.
bodyMedium: The VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance) program needs bilingual volunteer tax preparers for the 2026 filing season. Free IRS certification training is provided. Shifts run at libraries across Hennepin and Ramsey counties on Saturdays and weekday evenings. Somali, Hmong, and Spanish speakers are especially needed.
bodyHeavy:
The VITA program — short for Volunteer Income Tax Assistance — pairs trained volunteers with low- and moderate-income filers to prepare free federal and state returns. Twin Cities sites are staffing up for the 2026 filing season, and bilingual preparers are the single biggest need.

Volunteers complete IRS certification training before the season opens. The training is free, self-paced online, and ends with a short proficiency exam; sites run refresher sessions in person. Once certified, volunteers work 3- to 4-hour shifts at libraries across Hennepin and Ramsey counties on Saturdays and weekday evenings. Shifts are structured: a tax-certified preparer works with a filer while a quality reviewer checks every return before it's e-filed.

Somali, Hmong, and Spanish speakers are especially needed. Volunteers can request shifts near home, and rides are reimbursed for volunteers who commit to a regular site. Filers served through VITA claim an average of more than $2,000 in refunds and credits, including the Earned Income Tax Credit, that they otherwise might leave on the table.

items.name: Certified tax preparer
items.detail: Prepares federal and state returns for filers.

items.name: Site greeter
items.detail: Checks in filers; no tax certification required.

title: Garden Plots Available — Midtown Community Garden Near Lake Street
bodyLight: Four free 4x8 raised-bed plots available at Midtown Community Garden near Lake Street.
bodyMedium: Four 4x8 raised-bed plots are open at Midtown Community Garden near Lake Street. Free for first-year gardeners. Water access is on-site, and shared tools are available to borrow. Great for growing vegetables, herbs, or flowers. Contact the garden coordinator to claim a plot.
bodyHeavy:
Midtown Community Garden, a volunteer-run plot-share garden a few blocks off Lake Street, has four 4-foot-by-8-foot raised beds opening up for the growing season. All four are reserved for first-year gardeners, and the plots are free — no membership fee, no deposit.

The garden site has water spigots on-site, a shared tool shed with hand tools, and a compost bin. A volunteer coordinator walks new gardeners through basic raised-bed planting, watering, and the garden's simple rules (no chemical pesticides, keep your plot weeded, and pitch in on one shared workday per season). Plots are well-sized for a small vegetable or herb garden or a pollinator-friendly flower patch.

First-time gardeners get priority. Interested neighbors can reach the coordinator through the garden's signup form. Root Editorial has not independently verified the listing, but Midtown's coordinator is well known in the Lake Street gardener network.

items.name: 4x8 raised-bed plot
items.detail: Four available; reserved for first-year gardeners.

title: Free Cribs and Car Seats by Referral — 360 Communities in Burnsville
organizationName: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf
bodyLight: Free new cribs and car seats for qualifying Dakota County families by referral.
bodyMedium: 360 Communities in Burnsville distributes new cribs and car seats free to qualifying families. A referral from a case manager, home visitor, or WIC office is required. Items meet current safety standards, and installation help is available for car seats.
bodyHeavy:
360 Communities' Burnsville Resource Center distributes new cribs and car seats free to qualifying families across Dakota County. Items are brand-new and meet current federal safety standards; the program specifically avoids distributing used cribs or seats because the recall and crash history of second-hand items can't be verified.

Families come into the program through a referral from a case manager, home visitor, or WIC office. Referrals confirm eligibility and a basic safety review — that the family has a place for the crib to live, and a vehicle for the car seat to live in — before an item is released. The program coordinator can connect eligible families with other 360 Communities supports (food shelf, rent assistance, parenting classes) during the same visit.

Installation help is available for car seats. Certified passenger safety technicians check the installation in-vehicle before families drive away and walk caregivers through harness fit, seat angle, and the LATCH vs. seatbelt decision. The service is free and takes about 30 minutes per seat.

items.detail: Meets current safety standards; referral required.

title: Karen Language Tutors Needed for K–3 Students in Roseville and St. Paul
bodyLight: Karen-English tutors needed to work with K–3 students two hours a week. Stipend provided.
bodyMedium: The Karen Organization of Minnesota is recruiting bilingual Karen-English tutors for K–3 students at after-school programs in Roseville and St. Paul. The commitment is two hours per week on a flexible schedule. A small stipend is provided, and training plus curriculum materials are included.
bodyHeavy:
The Karen Organization of Minnesota is hiring bilingual Karen-English tutors to work with K–3 students at its after-school program sites in Roseville and St. Paul. The role supports kids who are newer to English with early-literacy work — letter sounds, sight words, read-aloud practice — in both Karen and English so students can build skills without losing their home language.

The commitment is two hours per week, with a flexible schedule across late-afternoon slots on weekdays. A small stipend is provided, and the organization covers training, curriculum materials, and on-site coaching from the program coordinator. Tutors do not need prior teaching experience, but fluent spoken Karen and enough written English to guide a young reader are required.

Background checks are run before the first shift. The organization will write service letters for students and can align schedules with university practicum hours for education majors. Interested neighbors can apply through the organization's website or call the Roseville office to start the conversation.

items.name: Karen-English tutor (K–3)
items.detail: Two hours per week, stipend provided.

title: Free Refurbished Laptops for Job Seekers in Workforce Programs
bodyLight: Free refurbished laptops for job seekers in CLUES, CAPI, and partner workforce programs.
bodyMedium: CLUES at the Minneapolis CareerForce Center is distributing refurbished laptops free to job seekers enrolled in workforce programs at CLUES, CAPI, or partner agencies. Laptops come with Windows, a carrying case, and a one-year warranty. Must be active in job search or training. One per household.
bodyHeavy:
CLUES at the Minneapolis CareerForce Center is handing out refurbished laptops to job seekers actively enrolled in workforce programs — including CLUES' own employment services, CAPI USA's career programs, and partner agencies across the metro. The program is meant to close the gap for job seekers who are ready to apply but don't have a reliable home computer.

Each laptop arrives with a clean install of Windows, a set of common productivity tools, a padded carrying case, and a one-year warranty from the refurbisher. Laptops are one per household, and a workforce case manager signs off on eligibility so the program can prioritize filers in active job search or training rather than general requesters.

Distribution runs at the CareerForce Center on East Lake Street during normal business hours. Recipients complete a short digital-literacy orientation — basics of secure password management, email, and job-application portals — before walking out with the machine. CLUES can connect recipients to low-cost home internet options at the same visit.

items.name: Low-cost internet referral
items.detail: CLUES can connect recipients at same visit.

title: Community Food Drive and Resource Fair at Sabathani
bodyLight: Sabathani hosts a food drive and resource fair with free lunch and 15+ service orgs.
bodyMedium: Sabathani Community Center hosts a combined food drive and resource fair at its South Minneapolis campus. Bring non-perishable items or stop in to browse tables from more than 15 local service organizations. Free lunch is served, kids' activities run through the afternoon, and interpretation is available in Somali and Spanish.
bodyHeavy:
Sabathani Community Center's spring food drive doubles as a resource fair, turning the South Minneapolis campus into a one-stop afternoon for food assistance, housing navigation, and health connections. Neighbors can bring non-perishable donations or simply stop in — no RSVP needed — and walk the aisle of tables staffed by more than 15 local service organizations.

Free lunch is served throughout the event. Representatives on-site include housing stability navigators, health insurance enrollers, employment programs, utility assistance, and early childhood services. Kids' activities — face painting, balloon animals, and a read-aloud corner — run in the gym so parents can work through the resource tables without rushing.

Interpretation is available in Somali and Spanish, and Sabathani's front desk can call in additional languages on request. The event is wheelchair-accessible and sits on two bus lines, with on-site parking and overflow parking across the street.

meta.deck: A free afternoon mixing food-drive donations with a resource fair of more than 15 service organizations — interpretation in Somali and Spanish, free lunch, and kids' activities throughout.

title: Know Your Rights Workshop — Tenants, Immigrants, and Workers
bodyLight: ILCM hosts a free evening workshop on tenant, immigration, and workplace rights.
bodyMedium: The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota runs a free evening workshop covering tenant rights, immigration basics, and workplace protections. Attorneys present, take questions, and stay after for short one-on-ones. Childcare is provided. RSVP preferred; walk-ins welcome. Materials in English, Spanish, Somali, and Hmong.
bodyHeavy:
The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota is running a free evening Know Your Rights workshop at its St. Paul offices, condensing three topics new arrivals most often ask about into one session: tenant rights, immigration basics, and workplace protections.

The tenant segment covers what a landlord can and cannot do in Minnesota — notices, repairs, deposits, and the eviction timeline. The immigration segment walks through the rights any person has during a home or workplace encounter with immigration officers, and when to call an attorney. The workplace segment covers wage and hour rules, retaliation protections, and the steps to take if a paycheck is short or a job is unsafe.

Attorneys present in plain language, take audience questions, and stay after for short one-on-one conversations about individual situations. Childcare is provided, and printed materials are available in English, Spanish, Somali, and Hmong. RSVP is preferred so ILCM can set out enough chairs and materials, but walk-ins are welcome.

meta.deck: A two-hour evening workshop covering tenant rights, immigration basics, and workplace protections, with attorney Q&A and one-on-one follow-ups afterward.

contacts.value: 450 N Syndicate St, Suite 200, St. Paul, MN 55104
contacts.label: ILCM offices

title: Karen New Year Celebration — Performances, Food, and Family Activities
bodyLight: Karen New Year: cultural performances, traditional food, and family activities. Free.
bodyMedium: The Karen Organization of Minnesota hosts its annual Karen New Year celebration in Roseville. Cultural performances, traditional food, weaving demonstrations, children's games, and live music run through the afternoon. Open to all community members. Free admission and free food.
bodyHeavy:
The Karen Organization of Minnesota's annual Karen New Year celebration brings the region's largest Karen community event to Roseville, and the organization opens it wide to neighbors — not just to the Karen families it serves. The afternoon is free, and traditional food is provided at no charge.

Performances begin at midday and run on a rolling schedule in the main hall: traditional dance groups, a youth choir, and drumming circles that invite audience participation. Around the hall, artisans lead Karen weaving demonstrations and hand out simple weaving kits for kids to try. A live music stage runs through the late afternoon with contemporary Karen musicians.

Family activities include games, a storytelling corner, and a craft table. The organization prints a short program in Karen and English, and staff are on hand to help first-time visitors find their way around. Parking is free and the site is wheelchair-accessible.

meta.deck: The region's largest Karen community celebration — five hours of performances, weaving demos, traditional food, and family activities, free and open to all neighbors.

title: Tenant Rights Clinic — Free Virtual Consultations with Housing Attorneys
bodyLight: HOME Line's free virtual tenant clinic offers one-on-one time with housing attorneys.
bodyMedium: HOME Line runs a free virtual tenant rights clinic for Minnesota renters. Each attendee gets a one-on-one consultation with a housing attorney. Topics include eviction defense, security deposit disputes, and lease questions. Spanish interpretation is available. Register at homelinemn.org or call 612-728-5767.
bodyHeavy:
HOME Line, Minnesota's statewide tenant hotline, hosts a free virtual tenant rights clinic for renters anywhere in the state. The clinic breaks a common bottleneck: rather than wait for a hotline callback, renters sign up for a reserved slot and meet one-on-one with a housing attorney by video or phone.

Attorneys cover the full range of tenant questions — eviction defense, security deposit disputes, lease language, habitability and repair complaints, and the process for responding to a landlord notice. Renters come with their documents in hand; the attorney reviews them live, explains next steps, and, where helpful, drafts a letter the tenant can send the same day.

Spanish interpretation is available on request, and HOME Line can arrange additional languages with enough notice. The clinic is open to any Minnesota renter regardless of income. Register through homelinemn.org or by calling the hotline at 612-728-5767.

meta.deck: A free statewide virtual clinic where Minnesota renters get a reserved one-on-one slot with a housing attorney — eviction defense, deposits, repairs, and lease questions.

title: Community Health Fair & Free Screenings
bodyHeavy:
NorthPoint Health, Hennepin Healthcare, and community clinics gather under one roof for a walk-in health fair in North Minneapolis. Visitors can get blood pressure, glucose, vision, and dental screenings at no cost, with flu shots available on site for anyone who wants one.

Interpretation runs in six languages throughout the day, so families can move between stations without waiting for a phone interpreter. Staff help attendees understand their numbers, flag anything that needs follow-up care, and connect uninsured visitors to sliding-scale clinics in the neighborhood.

Free lunch, children's activities, and a resource table with housing, food, and benefits navigators round out the fair. No appointment or insurance card required — organizers ask only that participants bring a medication list if they have one. Parking is free in the clinic lot; bus routes 5 and 19 stop within a block.
bodyMedium: Walk-in health fair with blood pressure, glucose, vision, and dental screenings plus flu shots. Six-language interpretation on site. Free food, kids' activities, and resource navigators from NorthPoint Health, Hennepin Healthcare, and neighborhood clinics. No insurance required.
bodyLight: Free health screenings and flu shots. Walk-in, six languages.

meta.deck: A walk-in clinic day in North Minneapolis with free screenings, flu shots, and six-language interpretation — bring your medication list, leave with a follow-up plan.

title: Adult Literacy Circle
bodyHeavy:
The Rice Street branch of the Saint Paul Public Library hosts a weekly adult literacy circle that pairs volunteers with learners one-on-one. Participants practice English reading, writing, and conversation at their own level — from first-letter recognition to job-application prose. Materials are provided, and there is no registration or fee.

The group meets in the branch's community room and welcomes drop-ins any week. Volunteers are fluent-English speakers trained by the library's adult services staff; several are bilingual in Spanish, Hmong, or Karen and can help learners bridge between their first language and English. Childcare is not provided, but the children's area is a short walk down the hall.
bodyMedium: Free weekly English literacy circle at the Rice Street library branch. One-on-one practice with trained volunteers at any level. Materials provided; drop-in welcome any week. Several volunteers speak Spanish, Hmong, or Karen.
bodyLight: Free English literacy circle. Drop-in weekly.

title: Citizenship Test Prep Class
bodyHeavy:
The International Institute of Minnesota runs a rolling 10-week citizenship test prep class that covers all 100 civics questions plus the reading and writing portions of the naturalization interview. New students can join any week of the cycle — instructors loop back through the material so nobody falls behind.

Classes meet in the evening so working adults can attend after shift. Interpreters are on hand in the most common languages of the Institute's caseload, and instructors can arrange additional language support on request. The program is free, and students who complete the cycle qualify for a no-cost mock interview with a legal services volunteer before their USCIS appointment.
bodyMedium: Ten-week rolling citizenship test prep at the International Institute of Minnesota. Covers all 100 civics questions plus reading and writing. Join any week; interpreters available; evening schedule for working adults. Free mock interview for completers.
bodyLight: Citizenship test prep. Interpreters available. Join anytime.

title: Morning Recovery Meeting
bodyHeavy:
A daily open recovery meeting runs every morning from 7 to 8 a.m. at a community space in South Minneapolis. The group is open — anyone with a desire to stop using is welcome, regardless of pathway or time in recovery. Coffee is provided, and the room is wheelchair accessible with a ramp at the side entrance.

The format is a lead-share followed by an open round-robin. Newcomers are greeted before the meeting starts and can speak, pass, or just listen. No sign-in, no attendance sheet. Organizers ask attendees to keep conversations inside the room inside the room — a basic anonymity commitment that makes it safe for people to speak openly about what they're working through.
bodyMedium: Open recovery meeting every morning 7–8 a.m. in South Minneapolis. Lead-share plus open round-robin, wheelchair accessible, coffee provided. All pathways welcome; newcomers greeted before start. Anonymity respected.
bodyLight: Daily recovery meeting 7–8am. All welcome.

title: Hmong New Year Celebration
bodyHeavy:
Hmong American Partnership invites the whole community to its annual Hmong New Year Celebration at its Arcade Street campus in the East Side of Saint Paul. Doors open mid-morning, and the day runs through the afternoon with pov pob (traditional ball toss), cultural performances, live music, and a full cycle of children's activities.

Admission is free and includes a traditional Hmong meal. Visitors can browse vendor tables with textiles, silver, and seasonal food; meet with HAP staff about workforce and benefits services; and spend time with elders at the storytelling corner. Organizers emphasize that this is a community-wide event — neighbors who are not Hmong are warmly invited and will find plenty of guides and interpreters to help them follow the program.

The Arcade Street lot offers free parking, and Metro Transit bus route 61 stops two blocks north of the campus. Strollers and wheelchairs have full access to the main floor; organizers ask that service animals be the only animals brought inside.
bodyMedium: Hmong American Partnership's annual Hmong New Year: pov pob, cultural performances, live music, traditional meal included with free admission. Vendor tables, children's activities, and elder storytelling corner. Bus route 61 stops two blocks away.
bodyLight: Hmong New Year at HAP. Performances, food, activities. Free.

meta.deck: HAP opens its Arcade Street campus for the community's biggest cultural day of the year — free admission, a traditional meal, and room for neighbors of every background.

title: Drop Off Donations at Lakeville's Spring Food Drive
bodyHeavy:
Messiah Community Food Shelf, run by 360 Communities in Lakeville, hosts its annual spring food drive in the Messiah Lutheran Church parking lot. Volunteers are stationed at a drive-through lane to help unload cars — donors stay in their vehicles if they prefer.

The shelf's most-needed items are canned proteins (tuna, chicken, beans), peanut butter, rice, and cooking oil. Diapers in sizes 4–6 and personal hygiene items are also welcome, as are unopened pet food bags for families struggling to keep their animals fed. Cash and check donations go directly to buying fresh produce and protein through the regional food bank, which stretches every dollar five-to-one.

This drive bridges the gap between winter holiday donations and the summer slump, when shelves across the south metro run thinnest. Messiah serves roughly 900 households a month; a single carload of donations covers about two days of shelf traffic. Organizers thank donors at the lane and hand out a list of ongoing volunteer opportunities for anyone interested in helping week to week.
bodyMedium: Annual spring food drive at Messiah Community Food Shelf in Lakeville — drive-through donations, volunteers unload for you. Most needed: canned proteins, peanut butter, rice, cooking oil. Cash donations stretch 5:1 through the regional food bank. Serves 900 households monthly.
bodyLight: Spring food drive in Lakeville. Drive-through, volunteers unload.

meta.deck: A drive-through food drive in Lakeville's spring donation gap — volunteers unload your car, cash donations stretch five-to-one through the regional food bank.

title: Craft & Connection Morning at Community Thread
bodyHeavy:
Community Thread in Stillwater opens its craft room for a weekly drop-in morning built around knitting, small-project crafting, and — as much as anything else — conversation. All skill levels are welcome, from people who've never held knitting needles to quilters looking for company while they work on their own projects.

Supplies are provided, including yarn, needles, paper crafts, and simple finished-in-an-hour kits for people who want to try something new. Coffee and tea stay on all morning. The room is bright and the tables are spaced for easy conversation across them.

Staff designed the program for neighbors who feel isolated on weekday mornings — new retirees, caregivers taking a break, people new to the area. There's no registration and no cost, and participants can arrive at any point during the two-hour window. Parking is free in the Community Thread lot on Orleans Street.
bodyMedium: Weekly drop-in craft morning at Community Thread in Stillwater. Knitting, paper crafts, quick finish-in-an-hour kits, plus coffee and conversation. All skill levels welcome; supplies provided. Designed for neighbors feeling isolated on weekday mornings.
bodyLight: Craft morning at Community Thread, Stillwater. Drop in.

title: Free Dental Day at Open Cities Health Center
bodyHeavy:
Open Cities Health Center offers a free dental day on the last Saturday of every month at its Saint Paul clinic. Patients can receive cleanings, fillings, and extractions with no insurance or ID required. The clinic's dentists, hygienists, and a rotating crew of volunteer providers handle as many patients as the day allows.

Doors open at 8 a.m. and the line forms early — organizers advise planning for a wait of two to four hours depending on the procedure needed. Coffee and light snacks are available, and a children's corner keeps kids occupied while parents wait. Interpretation is available in Somali, Karen, and Spanish; patients who need a different language can ask staff to arrange a phone interpreter.

The clinic encourages patients to bring a current medication list and, if possible, records of any recent dental work. Anyone turned away because the day fills up is placed on the top of the following month's list, so a wasted trip still counts toward faster care.
bodyMedium: Free monthly dental day at Open Cities Health Center in Saint Paul — cleanings, fillings, extractions, no insurance or ID needed. Doors at 8 a.m., expect a 2–4 hour wait. Interpretation in Somali, Karen, Spanish. Bring a medication list.
bodyLight: Free dental day at Open Cities. No insurance needed.

meta.deck: Free cleanings, fillings, and extractions one Saturday a month in Saint Paul — no insurance or ID, interpretation in three languages, bring your medication list.

title: Resume Workshop for New Americans
bodyHeavy:
CLUES workforce staff lead a bilingual resume and interview workshop at the organization's Lake Street offices in Minneapolis. The two-hour session covers how U.S. employers read resumes, how to translate international credentials into terms hiring managers recognize, and how to handle the most common interview questions without overpromising.

Participants can bring an existing work history in any language or start from scratch. Staff sit alongside attendees during the working portion and help each person leave with a first draft. Bilingual support is available in Spanish; instructors translate examples live as the workshop runs.

The workshop is free and open to anyone — no appointment, no documentation required. CLUES clients who attend are automatically eligible for a follow-up one-on-one with a workforce navigator, who can point them toward specific hiring events, trade certifications, and apprenticeships across the metro.
bodyMedium: Bilingual resume and interview workshop at CLUES Minneapolis. Covers translating international credentials, reading-between-the-lines for hiring managers, and common interview questions. Bring any work history or start fresh. Free, no appointment, Spanish and English.
bodyLight: Resume workshop for new Americans. Spanish and English.

title: Youth Basketball League Registration Open
bodyHeavy:
The North Community YMCA's spring youth basketball league is accepting registrations for the upcoming season. The program is open to kids ages 8–14, grouped into age brackets so everyone plays with peers of roughly their size and skill. Saturday games run at the Y's gym; practices meet twice a week on Tuesday and Thursday evenings.

Fees follow a sliding scale based on household income, and full scholarships are available — families are asked to ask, because staff have more scholarship money than they typically hand out. The league provides practice jerseys and game uniforms; players bring their own gym shoes and water bottles.

Coaches are background-checked volunteers from the neighborhood, most of whom played in this same league as kids. The program emphasizes consistent playing time over winning, so every rostered player sees at least half of every game. Registration closes two weeks before the first practice; late additions are taken on a case-by-case basis if rosters haven't filled.
bodyMedium: Spring youth basketball league at North Community YMCA, ages 8–14. Saturday games, Tu/Th practices. Sliding-scale fees with scholarships available. Jerseys provided. Neighborhood-volunteer coaches. Registration closes two weeks before the first practice.
bodyLight: Youth basketball league, ages 8–14. Registration open at the Y.

title: Somali Bantu Community Gathering
bodyHeavy:
The monthly Somali Bantu community gathering at Brian Coyle Center in Cedar-Riverside brings families together to share news, discuss needs, and connect with service providers in a culturally familiar setting. The gathering opens with a shared meal of traditional food; announcements and an open-floor discussion follow.

Interpretation runs between Maay Maay and English throughout the evening. A rotating set of service providers — food shelf staff, housing navigators, school liaisons, and immigration legal volunteers — staff a resource corner where attendees can ask questions quietly without taking the floor. Children are welcome, and a supervised play area keeps kids occupied while parents talk.

Organizers encourage neighbors who are not Somali Bantu to come once a year to listen and meet families. Seats along the back are reserved for guests who prefer to observe rather than participate in the discussion.
bodyMedium: Monthly Somali Bantu gathering at Brian Coyle Center. Shared meal, open-floor discussion, rotating service-provider resource corner. Maay Maay and English interpretation. Kids welcome with supervised play area. Guests welcome at the back.
bodyLight: Somali Bantu gathering at Brian Coyle. Food and services.
organizationName: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)

title: Free Car Seat Safety Check
bodyHeavy:
Dakota County Public Health sponsors a free drop-in car seat safety check at the Burnsville Fire Station. Certified Child Passenger Safety Technicians walk through each installation, point out common errors, and help drivers make corrections on the spot. No appointment is needed, but arriving in the first hour typically means a shorter wait.

Technicians can check any type of seat — infant, convertible, booster, or tether-equipped — and will flag seats that are expired or have been through a crash. Families who need a new seat and qualify for income-based assistance can leave with a replacement the same day; the fire station keeps a small stock for this purpose.

Drivers should bring the seat's instruction manual if available, the vehicle owner's manual, and the child if possible so techs can verify fit. The appointment typically runs 20–30 minutes per seat. The event is free and open to any family, regardless of Dakota County residency.
bodyMedium: Free drop-in car seat safety check at Burnsville Fire Station, sponsored by Dakota County Public Health. Certified technicians walk through installation, flag expired seats, offer replacements for qualifying families. Bring seat manual, vehicle manual, and child. 20–30 min per seat.
bodyLight: Free car seat check at Burnsville Fire Station.

title: Scott County Job Fair
bodyHeavy:
More than 30 Scott County employers will be on site at the Shakopee job fair hiring on the spot for manufacturing, healthcare, food service, warehouse, and logistics positions. The fair runs for four hours in the afternoon at a central Shakopee venue, with most employers set up to interview and extend offers the same day.

Attendees should bring multiple copies of a resume; CAP Agency staff stand by at a resume table to help applicants who arrive without one. Interpretation runs in Spanish and Somali, and a quiet corner is available for private conversations between applicants and employers. Attendees can also pick up free printed lists of every employer present, with the types of roles each company is hiring for.

To reach the fair without a car, a free bus shuttle runs every 30 minutes from the Prior Lake and Savage park-and-ride lots throughout the event. Childcare is not provided, but the venue is open to families and offers a quiet kid-friendly corner with books and crayons.
bodyMedium: Over 30 Scott County employers hiring on the spot in Shakopee — manufacturing, healthcare, food service, warehouse, logistics. Resume help on site, Spanish and Somali interpretation. Free shuttle from Prior Lake and Savage park-and-rides every 30 minutes.
bodyLight: Scott County job fair. 30+ employers. Hiring on the spot.

meta.deck: Thirty-plus Scott County employers hiring the same day in Shakopee — free shuttle from Prior Lake and Savage park-and-rides, resume help and interpretation on site.

title: Washington County Family Fun Day
bodyHeavy:
Basic Needs Inc. and Washington County host a free afternoon of games, crafts, and food at Lake Park in St. Paul Park. The day is designed for families — there is no admission fee, no registration, and no expectation that attendees know anyone going in.

Local service organizations staff a row of resource tables near the pavilion. Families can browse food, housing, childcare, and youth programming information in one spot, and the staff at each table are available for quick case-management questions. A local high school jazz band plays two sets during the afternoon.

Concessions are free while they last, with hot dogs, fruit, and water at the main table. Families are welcome to bring a picnic if they prefer. Strollers and wheelchairs have full access on the paved paths; the playground at the east end of the park is within sight of the pavilion.
bodyMedium: Free family afternoon at Lake Park in St. Paul Park. Games, crafts, free concessions, and a row of resource tables for food, housing, and childcare. Live jazz by a local high school band. No registration; strollers and wheelchairs welcome.
bodyLight: Family fun day at Lake Park, St. Paul Park. Free.

title: Parenting Through Immigration Stress—Support Group
bodyHeavy:
A biweekly bilingual support group at CLUES Minneapolis helps parents work through the family stress that comes with immigration uncertainty, paperwork cycles, and the everyday weight of raising kids in two cultures. A licensed therapist facilitates each session in a small-group format so every parent gets time to speak.

Sessions run in Spanish with English support available. There is no referral requirement and no cost. Childcare is provided during the group, and light snacks are set out so parents don't need to rush from work to make the start time. Group members agree to a basic confidentiality commitment at the first meeting of each cycle.

Topics are driven by what parents bring into the room — common themes include how to talk to kids about deportation fears without frightening them, how to manage the emotional labor of interpreting for the whole family, and how to find a minute of quiet when the phone never stops ringing.
bodyMedium: Biweekly Spanish/English support group for parents under immigration stress, led by a licensed therapist at CLUES Minneapolis. Free childcare, light snacks, no referral needed. Topics driven by parents — kids and deportation fears, interpreter fatigue, carving out quiet.
bodyLight: Immigration stress support group for parents. Biweekly at CLUES.

title: Li Nguyen
bodyHeavy:
Li Nguyen has coordinated more than 8,000 volunteer shifts at CEAP in Brooklyn Center since she joined in 2014. On a typical week she handles intake, matches volunteers to the roles that fit their schedules and languages, and quietly connects food shelf visitors with CEAP's housing navigators and ESL partners.

Her approach is simple and consistent: meet people where they are, listen first, and never let someone leave without knowing their next step. Over a decade she has built a network of bilingual volunteers who speak Vietnamese, Hmong, and Somali — a roster that now covers almost every shift the food shelf runs.

Li trains new volunteers herself. Her orientations run about 45 minutes and cover dignity-centered practice, confidentiality, and the handful of situations where a volunteer should immediately flag staff. Several of the full-time case managers at CEAP started as volunteers in her program.
bodyMedium: Li Nguyen has coordinated 8,000+ volunteer shifts at CEAP Brooklyn Center since 2014. She's built a bilingual volunteer network covering Vietnamese, Hmong, and Somali, and runs a dignity-centered orientation that several full-time case managers started in.
bodyLight: Volunteer coordinator at CEAP. 8,000+ shifts since 2014.

meta.deck: Eight thousand volunteer shifts, a bilingual roster covering three languages, and an orientation a CEAP case manager described as the first place they learned to listen — Li Nguyen's quiet decade.

title: Marcus Thompson
bodyHeavy:
Marcus Thompson is a case manager at People Serving People, the family shelter in downtown Minneapolis, where he works alongside families moving from emergency shelter into permanent housing. Over the last three years he has placed more than 150 families in stable homes across Hennepin County.

The work is relational and logistical at once. A typical day involves helping families assemble application packets, chasing down income verification, coaching landlords through the mechanics of a rapid rehousing voucher, and walking families through their first month in a new unit — where the utilities get paid, how to request repairs, which neighbors are already familiar faces.

His caseload skews toward families that other case managers called difficult to place: voucher holders with mixed documentation, large households, or families whose credit report carries the scars of a previous eviction. A People Serving People spokesperson says Marcus has a reputation internally for taking the hardest referrals and making them work.
bodyMedium: Marcus Thompson, case manager at People Serving People, has placed 150+ families into stable housing in three years. He specializes in the referrals other case managers call hardest — mixed documentation, large households, prior evictions — and walks families through their first month in-unit.
bodyLight: Case manager at People Serving People. 150+ families housed.

meta.deck: At People Serving People, the downtown family shelter, a case manager known for taking the hardest referrals has placed 150 families in three years.

person.name: Marcus Thompson
person.bio: Marcus works with families transitioning out of shelter into permanent housing across Hennepin County. Over three years he has placed more than 150 families — many with complex referrals other case managers passed on.
person.quote: The families other people call hardest are usually just the ones who most need someone to sit in the room with them.

title: Pimento Jamaican Kitchen
bodyHeavy:
Pimento Jamaican Kitchen is a Black-owned restaurant on Nicollet Avenue in South Minneapolis serving jerk chicken, oxtail, curry goat, ackee and saltfish, and a rotation of daily specials alongside fresh juices, house-made sorrel, and traditional Jamaican sides.

The kitchen caters community events and organizational meals — small plates for receptions, hot lines for board meetings, and full buffets for the sort of block gatherings that draw a hundred people. Orders can be placed by phone with a few days' notice; same-week availability depends on the calendar.

The restaurant sources ingredients from local farms when the season allows, hires staff from the surrounding neighborhood, and has become a meeting spot for small community groups that use the back dining area during quieter hours. Parking is on-street along Nicollet and in the shared lot two blocks south.
bodyMedium: Black-owned Jamaican restaurant on Nicollet Avenue. Jerk chicken, oxtail, curry goat, ackee and saltfish, plus fresh juices and house sorrel. Catering from small receptions to 100-person buffets. Sources local where possible; hires from the neighborhood.
bodyLight: Jamaican restaurant on Nicollet Ave. Catering available.

title: Hmong Village Shopping Center
bodyHeavy:
Hmong Village Shopping Center gathers more than 200 vendors under one roof on Johnson Parkway in Saint Paul's East Side. The food court alone spans a dozen stalls of prepared Hmong, Lao, Thai, and Vietnamese food; the grocery aisles carry fresh produce grown locally by Hmong farmers, live fish tanks, jars of fermented pastes, and specialty herbs rarely stocked at chain markets.

Beyond food, vendors offer traditional clothing, silver and jade jewelry, textiles for New Year celebrations, phone accessories, and a deep catalogue of cultural items — from ceremonial instruments to wedding accessories. Weekends draw the biggest crowds, particularly Saturday mornings when farmers first bring in produce.

Most stalls prefer cash, though more vendors accept cards every year. The market is a weekday community hub as much as a shopping destination: elders meet over coffee in the food court, students study between classes, and families pass through on a regular weekly loop. Parking is free and abundant in the main lot off Johnson Parkway.
bodyMedium: 200+ vendors at Hmong Village on Johnson Parkway: a dozen food stalls, locally grown produce, live fish tanks, traditional clothing, silver and jade, and specialty cultural items. A weekday community hub as much as a weekend market. Cash preferred; parking free.
bodyLight: 200+ vendors. Fresh produce, food, clothing, cultural items.

title: Midtown Global Market
bodyHeavy:
Midtown Global Market occupies the ground floor of the restored Sears building at Lake Street and Chicago Avenue. More than 40 small businesses operate inside — a tight mix of restaurants, grocers, artisans, and services representing cuisines from East Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and the Scandinavian communities that shaped the neighborhood before them.

The market is accessible by Metro Transit bus lines 5, 21, and 53, and the Midtown Greenway bike path runs directly north of the building. Community events fill the central atrium most weekends — cooking classes, music, holiday markets, and cultural celebrations that draw neighbors from across the metro.

Midtown Global Market anchors a nonprofit development model built to keep small, immigrant-owned businesses affordable in a commercial corridor that might otherwise price them out. Many vendors have been in place for more than a decade, and the market's manager says that stability is itself an attraction — visitors know the stalls they remember will still be there the next time they stop in.
bodyMedium: 40+ vendors at Lake St & Chicago Ave — restaurants, grocers, and artisans from East Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, and the Middle East. Bus routes 5, 21, 53 and the Midtown Greenway nearby. Nonprofit model keeps immigrant-owned businesses in place; many vendors have been there a decade-plus.
bodyLight: World cuisine, 40+ vendors. Lake St & Chicago Ave.

title: Karmel Mall
bodyHeavy:
Karmel Mall on Pillsbury Avenue South is the country's first and largest Somali mall — a commercial hub of restaurants, shops, prayer space, and money transfer services that has anchored South Minneapolis's Somali community for more than two decades.

Stalls carry traditional clothing (dirac, guntiino, men's suits tailored for Eid and weddings), groceries, henna, phone accessories, and cosmetics. A row of small restaurants and tea shops runs along one wing; hawala money transfer services operate from the central hallway, moving remittances to family overseas faster and cheaper than most banks.

The mall draws visitors from across the Midwest — families drive in from Iowa and the Dakotas to shop for weddings or Ramadan. For anyone new to the neighborhood, the mall is a direct and welcoming introduction to Somali culture; staff and shopkeepers are used to answering questions from curious first-time visitors.
bodyMedium: Karmel Mall on Pillsbury Ave — the country's first and largest Somali mall. Traditional clothing, groceries, henna, phone accessories, hawala money transfer, and a row of small restaurants. Draws families from across the Midwest for weddings and Ramadan shopping.
bodyLight: Somali shops, restaurants, money transfer on Pillsbury Ave.

title: Fatima Abdi
bodyHeavy:
Fatima Abdi runs the halal food shelf at Pillsbury United's Brian Coyle Center in Cedar-Riverside, where she and a small team serve more than 200 families every week. She arrived in Minnesota from Somalia in 2008 and spent her first years navigating the same services she now helps others access.

Her shelf is stocked differently than most — halal meats, East African staples, familiar spices, and produce calibrated to the cooking of the neighborhood. Fatima built many of the supplier relationships herself, calling through a regional distributor list and negotiating prices and delivery windows. Several vendors now donate weekly because of her persistence.

A second program Fatima started four years ago is a monthly cooking class where immigrant women learn food safety fundamentals and prepare for ServSafe certification — a credential that opens the door to catering and restaurant work. Graduates have gone on to launch small catering businesses, cook in school cafeterias, and staff the food shelf's own hot-meal program. A Pillsbury United spokesperson credits Fatima with building the pipeline between the food shelf and local restaurant hiring.
bodyMedium: Fatima Abdi runs the halal food shelf at Brian Coyle Center, serving 200+ families weekly. She built supplier relationships herself and started a monthly cooking class where immigrant women prep for ServSafe certification. Graduates now run catering businesses and staff school cafeterias.
bodyLight: Halal food shelf coordinator at Brian Coyle. 200+ families weekly.

meta.deck: Two hundred families a week, supplier relationships she built herself, and a pipeline from cooking class to ServSafe to restaurant kitchens — Fatima Abdi's halal food shelf at Brian Coyle is a workforce program, too.

title: David Xiong
bodyHeavy: David is an employment navigator at Hmong American Partnership who helps refugees and immigrants find their first U.S. jobs. He specializes in translating overseas credentials and work experience into formats that American employers understand, and he sits with clients through resume drafting, interview prep, and the first weeks on a new shift. Over the past year, he helped 89 clients secure employment in healthcare, manufacturing, and food service — most within ninety days of their first intake appointment. A HAP spokesperson described his approach as 'patient, methodical, and relentless,' noting that he follows up with employers months after a placement to resolve pay and scheduling issues before they become reasons to quit.
bodyMedium: David is an employment navigator at Hmong American Partnership helping refugees and immigrants land their first U.S. jobs. He translates overseas credentials for American employers and logged 89 placements last year across healthcare, manufacturing, and food service.
bodyLight: Employment navigator at HAP. 89 job placements in the past year.

title: Rosa Mendez
organizationName: CLUES (Comunidades Latinas Unidas En Servicio) – St. Paul
bodyHeavy: Rosa is a paralegal at CLUES who walks immigrant families through DACA renewals, TPS applications, and naturalization paperwork. She holds weekly office hours in both Spanish and English at the Maria Avenue office and has helped more than 300 families since 2020. She says her goal is to make the legal system less frightening for people who have already survived so much, and much of her work is simply translating the language of forms and federal notices into plain explanations families can act on. When filings are denied or delayed, she coordinates referrals to partner attorneys at the Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota and The Advocates for Human Rights so families are not left guessing about next steps.
bodyMedium: Rosa is a paralegal at CLUES helping immigrant families with DACA renewals, TPS applications, and naturalization. She holds weekly bilingual office hours and has guided more than 300 families through federal paperwork since 2020.

title: Tou Moua’s Garden
bodyHeavy: Tou Moua has tended a plot at the Midway Community Garden for 18 years, growing bitter melon, lemongrass, Thai peppers, and traditional Hmong herbs that are hard to find in neighborhood grocery stores. He shares surplus produce with neighbors on the walk home and teaches younger gardeners how to grow the vegetables their families ate in Laos. Garden coordinators say his rows are usually the first planted each spring and the last still producing in late October.
bodyMedium: Tou Moua has gardened at the Midway Community Garden for 18 years, growing traditional Hmong herbs and peppers hard to find in grocery stores. He shares surplus with neighbors and mentors younger gardeners in the techniques his family used in Laos.
bodyLight: 18-year Midway Community Garden grower. Traditional Hmong herbs and peppers.

person.quote: I grow what my mother grew. Young people here forget these plants — I want them to remember.

title: Maria’s Pupuseria
bodyHeavy: Family-run Salvadoran restaurant on Lake Street known for handmade pupusas, tamales, and horchata. Maria opened the shop six years ago with savings from her cleaning business and now employs five neighbors, most of whom live within walking distance. She donates leftover food to the Brian Coyle food shelf every Friday and hosts a free community meal on the first Sunday of each month. The shop has become a reliable after-school stop for students at nearby Roosevelt and South high schools.
bodyMedium: Family-run Salvadoran restaurant on Lake Street. Handmade pupusas, tamales, and horchata. Donates leftovers to Brian Coyle food shelf weekly and hosts a free community meal on the first Sunday of each month.
bodyLight: Salvadoran restaurant on Lake Street. Handmade pupusas and tamales.

title: James Fairbanks
bodyHeavy: James is a peer recovery specialist at NorthPoint Health who supports people navigating sobriety and reentry after incarceration. A North Minneapolis native, he draws on his own recovery journey to build trust with clients who are skeptical of institutions and have been let down by them before. He has mentored more than 60 men in the past two years, meeting many of them at the county jail's release door and staying with them through the first months of housing, sober-living rules, and job searches. A NorthPoint colleague said James's superpower is showing up when he says he will — a simple thing that builds more credibility than any program brochure.
bodyMedium: James is a peer recovery specialist at NorthPoint Health and a North Minneapolis native who has mentored 60+ men navigating sobriety and reentry. He meets clients at the jail release door and stays through the first months of housing and work.
bodyLight: Recovery specialist at NorthPoint. 60+ men mentored through reentry.

meta.deck: A NorthPoint peer recovery specialist meets clients at the jail release door and stays through the first months of housing, sober-living, and work.

person.name: James Fairbanks
person.bio: James supports people navigating sobriety and reentry, drawing on his own recovery journey.
person.quote: Showing up when you said you would — people who have been failed a hundred times can feel that immediately.

title: Oakdale Family Bakery
bodyHeavy: Vietnamese-owned bakery in Oakdale specializing in banh mi, pho, and French-style pastries. The owners, a husband-and-wife team, opened the shop after 15 years in restaurant work across the metro. They employ three part-time workers — two high-school students on after-school shifts — and donate day-old bread to the Open Cupboard food shelf three times a week. The Saturday morning pho line often wraps around the corner before the sign flips to open.
bodyMedium: Vietnamese-owned bakery in Oakdale known for banh mi, pho, and French-style pastries. The husband-and-wife owners employ three neighbors and donate day-old bread to Open Cupboard food shelf three times a week.

title: Amira Hassan

title: Emergency Shelter & Housing Help
bodyHeavy:
Emergency shelters, coordinated entry points, and housing assistance programs in the Twin Cities. If you are experiencing homelessness, start by calling Hennepin County Coordinated Entry at 612-348-9410 or Ramsey County at 651-266-4100. Coordinated entry is the primary way to be matched with both emergency shelter and longer-term housing programs, and a single intake call puts you on the list at multiple providers.

Shelter beds fill quickly — call early in the day. Most single-adult shelters begin taking reservations between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m., and walk-in slots typically fill by mid-afternoon on cold nights. Families with children receive priority placement and can usually be accommodated same-day through People Serving People or Mary's Place; unaccompanied youth under 24 should ask to be routed to YouthLink, which operates a dedicated drop-in center in downtown Minneapolis.

If you have nowhere safe to stay tonight and cannot reach coordinated entry, Hennepin County's after-hours line continues intake through the evening and can dispatch outreach workers to encampments, bus stations, and hospital waiting rooms. Survivors of domestic violence should call the statewide Day One hotline at 1-866-223-1111 for shelter routing that keeps addresses confidential.

Bring what identification you have, but none is required to receive emergency shelter. Caseworkers can help replace lost IDs, Social Security cards, and birth certificates through county-funded document programs once you are stabilized.
bodyMedium: Emergency shelters and housing help in the Twin Cities. Hennepin Coordinated Entry: 612-348-9410. Ramsey: 651-266-4100. Call early — beds fill quickly. Families and unaccompanied youth have dedicated routing.
bodyLight: Emergency housing: Hennepin 612-348-9410, Ramsey 651-266-4100.

title: When the Plow Doesn't Come: A Winter in Rural Aitkin
bodyHeavy:
Last winter's ice storm left dozens of seniors in McGregor and Palisade without cleared driveways for nearly a week. County road crews reached township roads first, but private driveways — many of them a quarter-mile long and banked by snow the plow had thrown back — were left to homeowners or whoever they could call. For residents on oxygen, dialysis, or home-delivered meals, that's not an inconvenience; it's an emergency.

A volunteer-run snow response network emerged from that storm, organized through Aitkin County's senior services office. Staff matched a list of known high-risk residents against a directory of neighbors who volunteered tractors, ATVs with plow attachments, and pickup trucks. The network now connects homeowners to plow-equipped neighbors within a three-hour radius, with a dispatcher working from the senior services office during active storms.

The program is strictly volunteer. No money changes hands, and the county does not verify equipment or insurance — an arrangement that would be unusual in a metro county but mirrors how rural townships have always handled mutual aid. Organizers say the trust is built over decades; the spreadsheet just formalizes it.

Since November the network has logged 240 clearing trips across Aitkin County, with heaviest demand in the Big Sandy Lake and Hill City areas. County staff say the model has drawn inquiries from senior services offices in Itasca, Crow Wing, and Kanabec counties considering similar programs ahead of next winter.
bodyMedium: After last winter's ice storm stranded rural seniors for days, a volunteer snow response network now connects them to plow-equipped neighbors through Aitkin County senior services. 240 clearing trips logged since November.
bodyLight: Aitkin's volunteer snow response network keeps rural seniors connected during storms.

meta.deck: How neighbors built what the county couldn't scale — a rural snow response network now running 240 trips a season across Aitkin County.

title: McGregor Ice Fishing Fundraiser Draws 400, Nets $18K for Food Shelf
bodyHeavy:
The 18th annual McGregor Ice Fishing Contest on Big Sandy Lake drew a record 400 registered anglers last weekend, raising $18,200 for the Aitkin Area Food Shelf. Proceeds will fund the shelf's mobile distribution route through the spring months, a weekly run that reaches seniors and families in Palisade, Hill City, and the lake communities outside McGregor.

Organizers said the contest benefited from a stable ice pack and calm weather that brought families out for the full Saturday event. Volunteers ran drilling rigs, a warming tent with donated hot dish, and a prize table stocked by local sporting-goods stores. A food shelf coordinator said the haul covers roughly four months of fuel and perishable costs for the mobile route and gives the organization a running start on the leaner summer months when donations typically dip.

The Aitkin County Board is collecting resident feedback on which rural corners of the county should be prioritized for fiber broadband expansion funded by the 2025 state infrastructure allocation. The online survey takes about 10 minutes and asks residents about current internet speeds, monthly cost, and the home services they rely on a reliable connection for.

A county spokesperson said responses will be weighted against existing coverage maps to identify underserved pockets where fiber extensions deliver the most value per dollar. Households without reliable service are the highest priority; the survey includes a mail-in option for residents who can't complete it online.

Paper copies are available at the courthouse front desk and at every branch of the Aitkin County library system. The survey closes in roughly two weeks; results feed a prioritization report the board plans to review before submitting the state grant application.

bodyHeavy:
Ruby's Diner is back open seven days a week after a kitchen fire last fall forced a four-month closure. The Palisade landmark kept its walleye sandwich on the menu and added a new rhubarb cream pie in honor of the volunteer fire department that fought the blaze.

The fire started in a grease trap and was knocked down before it spread beyond the back line, but smoke damage forced a full kitchen gut and rewire. The owner said the rebuild gave the family a chance to modernize the hood system and add a second fryer — small upgrades that paid for themselves in capacity on the first weekend back.

The community rallied during the closure with a GoFundMe that covered staff paychecks through the first two months. The diner is now hiring a dishwasher and a weekend line cook to handle returning lunch traffic.

The Aitkin Public Library has reopened its children's section after a six-week closure for roof repairs that followed a winter ice-dam leak. Storytime, the bean-bag reading corner, and the early-literacy kits are all back in their usual spots, and staff kept the summer reading signup running on its normal schedule during the closure.

About 200 picture books that got wet during the leak were replaced through a combination of insurance and donations from the Friends of the Library book sale.

Regular hours resume immediately. The Tuesday morning storytime returns next week, and the spring children's craft program picks up where it left off.

title: Coon Rapids Launches Warming Bus Pilot After Deadly Cold Snap
bodyHeavy:
After two people died of exposure in Coon Rapids and Blaine during January's cold snap, Anoka County Public Health launched a mobile warming bus pilot that circulates between the Coon Rapids Civic Center, the Blaine Walmart lot, and Riverdale Station from 8 p.m. to 6 a.m. on sub-zero nights. The bus is a retrofitted Metro Transit coach with seating, a small restroom, coffee, and an outreach worker on board who can make referrals to shelter, detox, or medical care.

Both deaths in January occurred within walking distance of open businesses, but neither person made it indoors. Public health officials say their review of the cases pointed to a gap the county had not previously acknowledged: Anoka's overnight shelter capacity is concentrated in a single site that requires sobriety and a brief intake, and there were no 24-hour drop-in spaces outside of hospital emergency rooms. The warming bus is intended to fill that gap without duplicating shelter infrastructure.

The route was chosen in consultation with Anoka County sheriff's deputies and outreach workers from Alexandra House and Lee Carlson Center, who flagged the three stops as locations where people had been found sleeping outdoors during previous cold snaps. Riders can stay on the bus for the full overnight loop or disembark at any stop.

The pilot runs through April and will be evaluated for permanent funding based on ridership, referrals made, and whether cold-related emergency calls decline in the served corridor. A county spokesperson said the evaluation framework was built with input from the Alexandra House case managers who will be fielding the downstream shelter requests, so the pilot is judged on whether it actually routes people to stable indoor options rather than on raw bus boardings alone.
bodyMedium: Anoka County's new warming bus pilot circulates overnight between Coon Rapids, Blaine, and Riverdale on sub-zero nights. Two deaths during January's cold snap catalyzed the program. Outreach workers on board can refer riders to shelter, detox, or medical care.
bodyLight: Mobile warming bus pilot launches after cold snap claimed two lives in Anoka County.

bodyHeavy:
Anoka County Community Action has expanded its SNAP application help to include Saturday mornings, a response to steady weekday demand that had pushed appointment waits past three weeks. Walk-ins are welcome from 9 a.m. to noon at the Anoka office, and staff can work through applications and renewals in English, Spanish, Somali, and Hmong.

A program navigator said about a third of weekday applicants were missing work to attend appointments, and the Saturday window is designed to take that pressure off. Applicants should bring a photo ID, proof of income from the last 30 days (pay stubs or a benefit award letter), and rent or mortgage information. Staff can still help applicants who arrive without every document, but the interview takes longer.

The expanded hours are a six-month pilot. If Saturday appointments consistently fill, the agency plans to make the schedule permanent and add a second outreach worker.

title: Dr. Amara Okonkwo — The Pediatrician Behind Anoka's School Clinic Program
bodyLight: Dr. Amara Okonkwo brought pediatric care into Anoka County schools.
bodyMedium: Dr. Amara Okonkwo launched Anoka County's first school-based health clinic at Jackson Middle in 2019. Six years later, four county schools have on-site pediatric care. "Kids were missing school for things that should take 15 minutes to fix," she says of the original pitch.
bodyHeavy:
Dr. Amara Okonkwo launched Anoka County's first school-based health clinic at Jackson Middle School in 2019. Six years later, four county schools have on-site pediatric care that handles everything from ear infections to vaccine boosters to mental health check-ins — without the family losing a half day of work.

The original pitch was simple: kids were missing school for things that should take 15 minutes to fix, and the lost-instruction cost was measurable on test scores. Okonkwo spent her first year in Anoka mapping absence patterns against ZIP codes and presenting the case to three school boards before the district agreed to pilot the Jackson Middle clinic.

Today the program employs four pediatricians, two nurse practitioners, and a behavioral-health clinician, and it operates on a sliding-scale fee structure that keeps the door open to uninsured families. Okonkwo is now working on an expansion into two Anoka-Hennepin elementary schools; the county board is expected to vote on the funding package later this spring.

title: Ramsey Library Offers Free Tax Prep Appointments
bodyLight: AARP Tax-Aide tax prep at Ramsey Library, Tuesdays and Thursdays through tax day.
bodyMedium: AARP Tax-Aide is offering free tax preparation at the Ramsey Library on Tuesdays and Thursdays through tax day. The service is open to residents of any age with low to moderate income. Appointments are required; walk-ins are turned away when the schedule fills.
bodyHeavy:
AARP Tax-Aide is offering free tax preparation at the Ramsey Library on Tuesdays and Thursdays through tax day. The service is open to residents of any age with low or moderate income — the AARP membership is not required — and IRS-certified volunteers handle the return start to finish.

Appointments are required and the schedule fills up a week or two in advance. Walk-ins without a slot are generally turned away. Taxpayers should bring a photo ID, Social Security cards for everyone on the return, last year's return, all W-2 and 1099 forms, and totals for any deductible expenses.

Tax-Aide can file both federal and Minnesota returns and can handle the property tax refund (Form M1PR) in the same appointment. Returns with rental property income, active-duty military overseas income, or bankruptcy filings are referred out to a paid preparer.
