title: Fridley Skate Park Reopens After Drainage Repair
bodyLight: Fridley Skate Park is open again after two months closed for drainage work.
bodyMedium: Fridley Skate Park is open again after a two-month closure for drainage work that reshaped the bowl's runoff channel. City parks staff said the old channel had been washing out the concrete edge and creating a lip that was hazardous on landings.
bodyHeavy:
Fridley Skate Park is open again after a two-month closure for drainage work that reshaped the bowl's runoff channel. City parks staff said the old channel had been washing out the concrete edge after every heavy rain and creating a sharp lip that was hazardous on landings.

The repair extended the channel, widened the drain grate, and patched about 40 feet of edge concrete. A parks manager said the park should hold up through a normal Minnesota winter without the seasonal patches that have become routine in recent years.

Hours are posted at the park; there is no fee. Helmets are strongly recommended.

title: Detroit Lakes' Summer Workforce Squeeze: When Staff Can't Afford to Live Here
bodyHeavy:
Three Detroit Lakes restaurants closed early last summer not because of slow business — but because they couldn't find staff who could afford to live in Becker County. Owners cited line cooks who commuted 45 minutes from Pelican Rapids, servers sharing a one-bedroom with three other workers, and a revolving door of seasonal hires who quit when they couldn't land housing close to the job.

Short-term rentals have gutted the long-term housing market in the county's tourism economy. A 2025 assessment by the Headwaters Regional Development Commission found that the number of homes listed on short-term rental platforms within the Detroit Lakes city limits had tripled since 2019, while long-term rental listings in the same period dropped by roughly half. Local employers, the school district, and Essentia Health's regional hospital are all competing with vacation platforms for the same housing stock.

A new coalition of employers is piloting employer-supported housing. The group — which includes the hospital, the school district, two resorts, and a manufacturing firm — has jointly purchased a 12-unit apartment building near downtown and plans to sublet units to workers at below-market rents tied to length of employment. A second coalition is exploring a master-lease arrangement with a rural developer.

City officials are also considering a short-term rental cap or licensing fee, though similar proposals have stalled in other northern Minnesota tourism towns. A workforce housing study commissioned by the county is due this summer. A Detroit Lakes city council member said the council is watching how a similar cap performs in Grand Marais before committing to an ordinance here — the goal is a rule that protects long-term housing supply without driving every small-scale host out of business.
bodyMedium: Detroit Lakes' summer economy runs on workers who can't afford to live in the county. Short-term rentals have gutted long-term housing stock. A new employer-housing coalition is piloting a 12-unit building and the county's workforce study is due this summer.
bodyLight: Becker County's tourism economy is colliding with its housing shortage.

bodyHeavy:
The 32nd annual Frazee Turkey Trot 5K kicks off at Town Square on Saturday at 9 a.m. Registration is $20 and every dollar — plus the proceeds from the post-race pancake breakfast — benefits the Frazee Food Pantry. The kids' half-mile fun run is free and goes off 15 minutes before the main race.

The course is a flat out-and-back loop through downtown and along the lake path, suitable for strollers and jog-walkers. Race-day registration opens at 7:30 a.m. at the pavilion; online pre-registration closes Friday night. All participants get a long-sleeve shirt while supplies last and a finisher's ribbon.

A volunteer organizer said last year's event raised just over $9,000 for the pantry and drew roughly 350 runners. The organizing committee is still looking for a few course marshals — volunteers can sign up at the pavilion on race morning.

title: County Seeks Public Comment on Wastewater Grant Proposal
bodyLight: Comment on the Lake Park wastewater grant proposal before the deadline.
bodyMedium: Becker County is applying for a $4.1M state grant to expand the Lake Park wastewater treatment plant. Public comments are being accepted for the next two weeks at the county administrator's office, by email, or through the online comment form linked on the county website.
bodyHeavy:
Becker County is applying for a $4.1 million state grant to expand the Lake Park wastewater treatment plant. The expansion would roughly double the plant's peak capacity, address a 2024 state regulatory notice about effluent nitrogen levels, and add headroom for the subdivisions in planning north of town.

Public comments are being accepted for the next two weeks at the county administrator's office, by email, or through the online comment form linked on the county website. A county engineer said comments will be bundled into the grant application as part of the required community-engagement documentation; comments raising specific technical concerns are more likely to shape the final design than general support or opposition.

A public information meeting is scheduled at the Lake Park city hall before the comment window closes. Written comments are weighted the same as oral comments at the meeting.

meta.deck: The county has about two weeks to gather public comment before submitting a $4.1M state grant application to double the Lake Park plant's peak capacity.

title: Mahube-Otwa Resumes Weatherization Waitlist
bodyLight: Mahube-Otwa's free home weatherization waitlist reopens for Becker County.
bodyMedium: Mahube-Otwa Community Action has reopened its home weatherization waitlist for Becker County. Income-qualifying homeowners and renters can get insulation, air sealing, and heating upgrades at no cost. Expected wait time is eight to fourteen months from application to installation.
bodyHeavy:
Mahube-Otwa Community Action has reopened its home weatherization waitlist for Becker County after roughly a year of closed intake. Income-qualifying homeowners and renters can receive insulation, air sealing, heating-system tune-ups or replacement, and minor repair work at no cost. Expected wait time is eight to fourteen months from application to installation.

Eligibility is tied to household income at or below 60 percent of the state median — roughly $52,000 for a family of four — and applicants already enrolled in SNAP or LIHEAP are automatically income-eligible. Renters need written landlord approval before work begins; the program includes a template landlord letter to speed that step.

A program coordinator said the scope of work averages around $8,000 per home and typically cuts annual heating bills by 15 to 25 percent. Applications are accepted online or by phone. The waitlist is expected to fill within the next quarter; applying now preserves a place in line even if paperwork is completed later.

title: The Bait Box: How a Detroit Lakes Shop Became the Town's Unofficial Job Board
bodyMedium: The Bait Box on Washington Avenue has sold minnows for 47 years — and over the past decade, its front window has become Detroit Lakes' most reliable job board. Seasonal employers tack up handwritten cards because, as the owner puts it, it's the one place every local stops in.
bodyLight: The Bait Box's window is Detroit Lakes' most reliable job board.
bodyHeavy:
The Bait Box on Washington Avenue has sold minnows, crawlers, and ice-fishing tackle for 47 years. Somewhere along the way, without anyone planning it, the shop's front window also became the most reliable job board in Detroit Lakes. On any given weekday the glass carries a dozen handwritten three-by-five cards — resort cabin cleaners for the summer, short-order cooks, dock hands, a landscape crew looking for people who don't mind mosquitoes.

The owner, who opened the shop in the late 1970s and still works the counter most mornings, says it started with resort owners she knew from the lake. When their help didn't show up, they'd leave a card near the minnow tanks rather than drive into town to place a newspaper ad. Job-seekers noticed. Word spread. Now the window is taped edge-to-edge through the spring hiring rush, and local employers say a posting here reaches the right people faster than anything online.

The shop takes no cut — postings are free — and the owner sorts out expired cards once a week. The arrangement has survived three national recessions, a pandemic, and the arrival of every hiring app on the market, mostly because the shop sits on the route almost every working person in town takes at some point during the week. Walk in for night crawlers at 6 a.m. and you leave knowing which resort is short a housekeeper by Friday.

meta.deck: A 47-year-old minnow shop on Washington Avenue became the town's most reliable hiring hub, one handwritten index card at a time.

title: Becker County Emergency Resource Directory
bodyMedium: Frontline resources for housing, food, mental health, and emergencies in Becker County. Call ahead when possible — intake windows change week to week and some services require a short screening before an appointment can be scheduled.
bodyLight: Frontline contacts for housing, food, and crisis help in Becker County.
bodyHeavy:
This directory collects frontline contacts for Becker County residents facing housing instability, food insecurity, mental-health crises, or other emergencies. Each agency listed here confirmed its phone line within the past 30 days. Most accept walk-ins during weekday business hours; the crisis line is staffed around the clock.

Becker County Human Services is the starting point for public benefits, emergency assistance, and child and family services. Mahube-Otwa Community Action covers energy assistance, housing stabilization, and Head Start. The Detroit Lakes Food Shelf operates appointment-based distribution and can connect clients to other food programs in the region. The Lakes Crisis & Resource Center responds to domestic violence and sexual assault and also provides shelter.

Call ahead when possible. Intake windows shift week to week and some services require a short screening before an appointment is scheduled. Interpretation is available through all four agencies on request.

bodyHeavy:
The Detroit Lakes Senior Center has moved its weekly lunch menu and meal sign-up to the city website, replacing a paper flipbook that lived on the entry table for years. Staff say the switch was driven by caregivers and adult children who wanted to see the menu without a phone call — many coordinate rides and shared meals for older parents and asked for a version they could check from work.

Lunches are still served each weekday at noon, with a suggested donation of $5. Same-day sign-up remains welcome by phone when seats are available, and the center will continue printing a paper copy for members who prefer it. The online menu lists the week's entrées, sides, and any heart-healthy or low-sodium flags.

title: Ice Road Season Extended on Big Detroit Lake
bodyLight: A late cold snap keeps Big Detroit Lake's ice road to the point open for an extra week.
bodyMedium: A late-season cold snap has extended the plowed ice road from the public access to the point on Big Detroit Lake by roughly another week. Local volunteers managing the route ask drivers to stick to marked lanes, avoid pressure ridges near the south shore, and haul out their own trash when leaving fish-house sites.
bodyHeavy:
A late-season cold snap has extended the plowed ice road running from the public access to the point on Big Detroit Lake by roughly another week beyond the usual closing target. The volunteer crew that maintains the route met over the weekend and agreed conditions were still safe for light vehicle traffic, based on thickness readings taken at three spots along the run.

Drivers should stick to the marked lanes, stay off pressure ridges near the south shore, and avoid the soft spot that has opened near the old crib dock. The crew asks anyone still using fish houses on the lake to begin pulling them in this week — even with the extended window, the next warm-up will bring the season to a hard close.

This is a volunteer-maintained route. There is no official ice-road authority. Drivers use the road at their own risk, and the crew asks that everyone haul out their own trash when leaving a fish-house site.

title: Highway 34 Repaving Pushed Back One Month
bodyLight: MnDOT pushes the Highway 34 repaving project east of Detroit Lakes back roughly one month.
bodyMedium: MnDOT has pushed the Highway 34 repaving project east of Detroit Lakes back roughly one month to let the subgrade dry out after a wet spring. Once crews do mobilize, drivers should expect single-lane closures during daylight hours and short delays through the work zone.
bodyHeavy:
The Minnesota Department of Transportation has pushed the Highway 34 repaving project east of Detroit Lakes back by roughly a month, citing a wet spring that has kept the subgrade from drying out enough to hold new pavement. The delay pushes the start window deeper into the season and tightens the schedule for finishing before the summer tourism traffic increase.

Once crews do mobilize, drivers should expect single-lane closures during daylight hours with flagging crews directing alternating flow, and short delays through the work zone at peak commute times. Commercial traffic and school-bus routes have been notified directly. MnDOT says it will publish an updated closure calendar on the project page once a firm start date is set and asks drivers to monitor 511mn.org for day-of conditions.

title: Bemidji's Cold-Weather Shelter Ran Out of Beds Every Night in February
bodyHeavy:
Bemidji's only adult overflow shelter hit capacity every single night in February, turning away dozens of people to sleep in cars, hospital waiting rooms, or the doorways of closed storefronts along Paul Bunyan Drive. Staff at the shelter, which is operated by a partnership between local churches and Beltrami County social services, said they counted 37 nights in a row on which every cot was full by 9 p.m.

Community organizers are pushing the city and county to open a second site in partnership with Red Lake Nation's social services — a collaboration that has existed informally for years but never at this scale. A significant share of shelter guests are enrolled members of Red Lake, Leech Lake, or White Earth, and advocates say a jointly staffed site could shorten the travel distance for people moving between reservation services and the city, while also bringing cultural and health resources that the existing shelter cannot provide.

The proposal under discussion would repurpose a vacant wing of a former assisted-living facility, adding roughly 40 beds and a daytime drop-in space. Funding would come from a mix of state cold-weather shelter grants, tribal health dollars, and Beltrami County levy funds. County commissioners have asked staff to return with a cost estimate and operating plan before a vote later this spring.

A county spokesperson said the timing matters: the shelter grant cycle closes in early summer, and a proposal that clears committee in time would be eligible for state matching funds that would otherwise go elsewhere. Advocates also emphasized that a second site is not just a winter question — the existing shelter runs at or near capacity well into the shoulder seasons.

In the meantime, organizers have set up a volunteer driver network to take people turned away at the shelter door to hospital lobbies and all-night gas stations considered safer than the street. The network runs on a group text with about two dozen drivers; anyone with a reliable vehicle and a clean background check is welcome to join.
bodyMedium: Bemidji's only adult overflow shelter turned people away on every night of February, and organizers are pressing the city and county to open a second site jointly with Red Lake Nation. The proposal would repurpose a vacant assisted-living wing and add roughly 40 beds plus a daytime drop-in.

title: BSU Pow Wow Returns to Sanford Center This Weekend
bodyMedium: The 43rd Annual Bemidji State University Pow Wow returns to the Sanford Center with two grand entries Saturday, one at 1 p.m. and one at 7 p.m. Free and open to the public. Regalia, dancing, drum groups, and food vendors all day.
bodyLight: 43rd BSU Pow Wow at Sanford Center this weekend. Free, open to all.
bodyHeavy:
The 43rd Annual Bemidji State University Pow Wow returns to the Sanford Center this weekend with two grand entries on Saturday — one at 1 p.m. and another at 7 p.m. The event is free and open to the public, drawing dancers, drum groups, and families from across the region.

Between grand entries the arena floor stays open for intertribal dancing, and food vendors line the concourse with fry bread, wild rice soup, and Indian tacos. Vendor tables around the perimeter feature beadwork, quillwork, and regalia materials for sale. A designated seating area is set aside for elders, and a staff table near the main entrance can help first-time visitors with etiquette questions.

Parking is free at the Sanford Center lot. Organizers ask attendees to arrive at least 30 minutes before grand entry to find seating, especially for the evening session, which tends to fill. Photography of individual dancers requires permission from the dancer; the emcee will announce restrictions for specific songs.

title: Kelliher Community Clinic Opens Walk-in Mental Health Hours
bodyMedium: Kelliher Community Clinic now offers walk-in mental health screening Thursdays from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. Licensed therapists are on-site, a sliding fee scale is available, and no appointment is required. Interpretation is available on request.
bodyLight: Walk-in mental health hours Thursdays 10 a.m.–3 p.m., Kelliher Community Clinic.
bodyHeavy:
Kelliher Community Clinic has opened a weekly walk-in mental health screening window on Thursdays from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. No appointment is required. Licensed therapists are on-site for brief assessments, crisis support, and referrals into longer-term care, and a sliding fee scale is available for uninsured or underinsured patients.

Clinic staff say the walk-in hours are aimed at reducing the gap between someone deciding they need help and actually getting in a door — a gap that, under the previous appointment-only model, often stretched into weeks. The session is confidential and does not create a permanent record unless the patient chooses to continue into ongoing care. Interpretation is available on request; patients should mention preferred language at the front desk.

Anyone in immediate crisis should call or text 988 before coming in. The walk-in clinic is not a replacement for 911 or emergency room care in life-threatening situations.

sourceAttribution.attribution: Clinic announcement relayed by Root Editorial

title: Bemidji Community Food Shelf Seeks Saturday Volunteers
bodyMedium: Bemidji Community Food Shelf needs four regular Saturday volunteers for the morning distribution shift, 8 a.m. to noon. Volunteers help set up client-choice aisles, stock, and carry out. Must be able to lift 30 pounds. Orientation provided.
bodyLight: Saturday distribution volunteers needed, Bemidji Community Food Shelf.
bodyHeavy:
Bemidji Community Food Shelf is looking for four regular Saturday volunteers to anchor the morning distribution shift from 8 a.m. to noon. Saturday is the pantry's busiest distribution window — it's the one that working families lean on most — and the weekend crew has been running two or three people short since the start of the year.

Volunteers help set up the client-choice aisles before doors open, keep shelves stocked as families move through, and carry groceries out for seniors and anyone who asks. Orientation is a single two-hour session held on a weekday evening, covering client privacy, food-safety basics, and how the client-choice model works. Volunteers should be able to lift 30 pounds and stand comfortably for most of the shift. The shelf welcomes volunteers age 16 and up; under 18 needs a parent or guardian's sign-off.

Shifts run year-round. The food shelf asks new volunteers to commit to at least one Saturday a month for the first three months, since scheduling consistency is what actually helps staff plan.

title: Jessica Roy Built Bemidji's First Indigenous-Run Doula Collective
bodyMedium: Jessica Roy (Red Lake Nation) founded Wáasa Birth Collective after her own traumatic hospital birth a few years ago. The collective has now supported 63 births across Beltrami and Clearwater counties — most for Indigenous families — with zero NICU transfers reported to date.
bodyLight: Jessica Roy's Wáasa Birth Collective supports Indigenous families through birth.
bodyHeavy:
Jessica Roy, an enrolled member of Red Lake Nation, founded Wáasa Birth Collective after her own traumatic hospital birth a few years ago. She had gone in expecting support; she came out determined that no one in her community would go through what she did alone. The collective now trains and deploys Indigenous doulas across Beltrami and Clearwater counties and has supported 63 births to date — most for Indigenous families — with zero NICU transfers reported.

The collective operates on a sliding scale and never turns away a family for inability to pay. Doulas accompany clients through prenatal visits, labor, and the immediate postpartum window, and they bring cultural practices — a blessing, the burning of cedar, songs in Ojibwemowin — into rooms where those practices have historically been unwelcome. Roy says the point isn't to replace medical care but to make sure someone in the room is listening to the person giving birth.

Wáasa also runs monthly community-circle nights where new and expecting parents gather with elders and other doulas. The sessions are free, open to Indigenous and non-Indigenous families alike, and always include a meal. Roy says the circle is where a lot of the collective's best recruiting happens — people who come once as clients often come back as volunteer supports, and a few have gone on to doula training themselves.

title: Beltrami County Crisis & Support Directory
bodyMedium: Core contacts for mental health, shelter, and tribal services in Beltrami County. Numbers verified within the past 30 days. Call 988 or 911 if you or someone you're with is in immediate danger.
bodyLight: Core contacts for mental health, shelter, and tribal services in Beltrami County.
bodyHeavy:
This directory collects the core frontline contacts for mental-health, shelter, and tribal services in Beltrami County. Every number listed here was verified within the past 30 days. Call 988 or 911 first if you or someone you're with is in immediate danger; the entries below are for ongoing or next-day support.

Beltrami County Health & Human Services handles public benefits, mental-health referrals, child and family services, and coordinated entry for housing programs. Evergreen Youth & Family Services operates youth shelter and related supports. Red Lake Nation Family Services is the primary family-services contact for Red Lake enrollees and a useful first call for anyone navigating tribal-county coordination. Bemidji Community Food Shelf operates a client-choice pantry and can connect families with broader food access.

Most agencies listed here accept walk-ins during weekday business hours and offer interpretation on request. Ask the front desk if you need help connecting across agencies — staff regularly make warm hand-offs to save families the work of repeating their story.

title: BSU Hockey Arena Hosting Free Community Skate Every Sunday
bodyLight: Free community ice skating Sundays 2–4 p.m. at Sanford Center. Rental skates available.
bodyMedium: Free community ice skating runs every Sunday from 2 to 4 p.m. at the Sanford Center, Bemidji State's home ice. Rental skates are available for a small deposit. The session is open to all ages — beginners welcome, helmets encouraged for kids.
bodyHeavy:
Free community ice skating runs every Sunday afternoon from 2 to 4 p.m. at the Sanford Center, Bemidji State University's hockey arena. The session is open to all ages and skill levels. Beginners are welcome, and coaching volunteers are usually somewhere on the ice to help new skaters find their feet.

Rental skates are available at the rink-side booth for a small cash or card deposit, returned when the skates come back. Helmets are encouraged for children and available for loan. The concession stand is open for the session. Organizers ask parents to keep younger kids in the designated beginner lane during busy stretches and to pay attention to the ice crew during the mid-session scrape.

The program is supported by BSU Athletics and a rotating group of local sponsors. Sessions are weather-independent — the rink is indoor and runs on the regular hockey schedule the rest of the week.

title: Blackduck Public Schools Extend Late Bus Through End of April
bodyLight: Blackduck schools extend the after-school activity bus through the end of April.
bodyMedium: Blackduck Public Schools extended its after-school activity bus service through the end of April to cover the spring sports and club season. The late bus runs the same route as the regular afternoon route, departing roughly two hours after final bell.
bodyHeavy:
Blackduck Public Schools has extended its after-school activity bus service through the end of April, giving student athletes and club members a ride home that lines up with the end of the spring sports season. The late bus runs the same route as the regular afternoon route, departing from the school roughly two hours after the final bell.

The service is funded through a mix of district activity budget and a small community donor pool. Families whose students plan to use the late bus should let the front office know by the morning of, so the driver knows how many stops to plan. Students who miss the late bus should call home from the front office rather than start walking — the district still has staff on site during that window.

title: Tribal Tax Credit Workshop at Red Lake Nation College
bodyLight: Tribal tax credit workshop Saturday morning at Red Lake Nation College. Free.
bodyMedium: A free tribal tax credit workshop takes place Saturday at Red Lake Nation College. Volunteer preparers walk attendees through federal and tribal-specific credits, including earned-income and child tax credits. Bring photo ID, Social Security cards for the household, and last year's return if available.
bodyHeavy:
A free tribal tax credit workshop takes place Saturday morning at Red Lake Nation College, hosted by a volunteer team of tax preparers and tribal-finance staff. The session focuses on federal credits that tribal members often leave unclaimed — earned-income credit, child tax credit, and education credits — along with tribal-specific deductions and reporting questions that come up every year.

Attendees should bring a photo ID, Social Security cards or ITINs for every household member, last year's return if available, and any 1099 or W-2 forms from the current tax year. Walk-ins are welcome; organizers ask anyone who expects a complex return — self-employment, multiple states, rental income — to call ahead so a preparer with the right training is scheduled in.

The workshop is free and open to enrolled members and non-enrolled community members alike. Snacks and coffee are provided.

title: MN Statewide Hotlines
bodyLight: Critical statewide numbers for anyone in Minnesota.
bodyMedium: Critical statewide hotlines for anyone in Minnesota: 211 connects callers to local help for food, housing, and benefits. 988 is the 24/7 suicide and crisis line. MN Day One is the statewide domestic violence and sexual assault line.
bodyHeavy: A pocket reference of the statewide hotlines every Minnesotan should know. 211, operated through United Way, is the catch-all connection line for food, housing, utility, and benefits help — calls are routed to the nearest participating agency and the service runs 24 hours a day. 988 is the national Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, available by call or text, and will route the caller to a Minnesota-based crisis counselor whenever possible. MN Day One is the statewide domestic-violence and sexual-assault line, connecting callers to shelters and advocates across Minnesota. For immediate danger, call 911 first; the hotlines above are for next-step navigation and longer-form support.

title: Free Tax Help Through the Filing Deadline
bodyHeavy:
Free tax preparation is available statewide through VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance) and AARP TaxAide. Most sites serve anyone earning under $64,000 per year, with some AARP sites open to filers of any income if you're 60 or older. Volunteer preparers are trained and IRS-certified; they can file federal and state returns, including most common credits like the Earned Income Tax Credit and the Minnesota Working Family Credit.

Appointments fill up quickly as the filing deadline approaches. Most sites require you to book in advance through the MN Revenue site finder; a handful offer walk-in hours on specific days. Bring photo ID, Social Security cards for everyone on the return, all W-2 and 1099 forms, last year's tax return if you have it, and any documents related to healthcare coverage or education expenses. Renters should bring a Certificate of Rent Paid from their landlord to claim the Renter's Property Tax Refund.

If you're filing a language other than English, ask when you book — many sites have interpreters or bilingual preparers for Spanish, Somali, Hmong, Vietnamese, and a handful of other languages. For questions not answered on the site finder, MN Revenue's taxpayer help line can walk through which sites near you fit your situation.
bodyMedium: Free tax prep is available statewide through VITA and AARP TaxAide. Most sites serve filers earning under $64,000; AARP sites also serve anyone 60 or older at any income. Bring photo ID, SSN cards, W-2s and 1099s, last year's return, and a Certificate of Rent Paid if you rent.
bodyLight: Free tax help available statewide through VITA and AARP TaxAide.

title: Ice Safety: Know Before You Go
bodyLight: MN DNR recommends 4+ inches of clear ice for foot travel. Call the DNR hotline before heading out on unfamiliar lakes.
bodyMedium: The Minnesota DNR recommends at least four inches of new, clear ice for foot travel and thicker for vehicles. Ice thickness varies across a single lake, especially near inlets, springs, and current. Anglers should call local tackle shops and the DNR information line before venturing onto unfamiliar water.

title: Free Radon Test Kits Available from MDH
bodyLight: MN Department of Health offers free radon test kits to homeowners. Over 2 in 5 MN homes have elevated radon levels.
bodyMedium: The Minnesota Department of Health distributes free short-term radon test kits to homeowners who have never tested. Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas and is the leading cause of lung cancer in non-smokers. Roughly two in five Minnesota homes show elevated levels; testing is quick and mail-in.
